critiques.persona: Saanvi Rao, Editorial Art Director — brand identity, typography & color
critiques.overallImpression: You have two beautiful halves that have never been introduced to each other. The typographic spine — Cormorant Garamond, weight 400, all-caps, ~2px tracking, in sage on white — is a genuinely sophisticated, editorial choice (it reads like a fashion masthead, Kinfolk/Cereal territory), and the love-story copy is specific, warm, and well-set on a disciplined 60–70 character measure. That's real craft. But the *decorative and color system* is pure stock template, and it is actively at war with your own photography. The single loudest fact in the whole site: your watercolor florals are blush-pink, cream and butter-yellow English-cottage-garden blooms, while your actual proposal images are crisp Cycladic cobalt-blue-and-whitewashed-stone — the blue-domed Church of the Seven Martyrs against the Aegean. Those two palettes sit on nearly opposite sides of the color wheel (warm rose/butter vs. cool cobalt). The sage accent you chose to customize the theme agrees with the flowers and fights the photos. So the most ownable thing you have — a 36-hour-flight surprise proposal on a Greek island — never makes it into the visual identity; it's trapped in the photos and the prose. Right now this reads as 'elegant default template,' when the raw material is here to make it read as 'unmistakably Meghan and Owen.' The good news: almost everything that matters is reachable inside Zola through theme, color, font, image, content and copy choices.
strengths: The type pairing is correct and confident. Cormorant Garamond for display against Circular for body/UI is a classic high-contrast serif-display / neutral-sans split — it gives you elegance up top and clean readability in the paragraphs. This is the kind of pairing a paid designer would reach for, not a default instinct.

There's a real two-color TEXT discipline that shows intent: sage Cormorant for the wordmark, H1s, date and OUR LOVE STORY heading, versus near-black (#0E0E0E) Circular for running copy. Reserving the accent color for display type and keeping body text neutral is exactly the right hierarchy move — most couples color everything or nothing.
The all-caps + ~2px tracking on the Cormorant headings is a tasteful, magazine-masthead gesture. At the large wordmark and H1 sizes it earns its keep and gives the whole site a quiet editorial poise.
Generous whitespace and a narrow ~60–70ch measure on the story paragraphs. That measure is textbook-correct for readability and it makes genuinely strong writing feel considered rather than dumped.

The hero's restraint — a full-bleed three-panel band with NO text or date overlaid — is a defensible editorial choice. Letting the image carry the top of the page without slapping a script font over it is more sophisticated than 90% of wedding sites.

The story copy itself is the best asset on the site: specific, funny, and structured (the 'butterfly effect' roommate setup, 'the easiest yes of all time'). It gives the whole identity something true to be built around.

issues.title: The florals fight the photography — warm cottage-garden palette vs. cool Cycladic blue-white
issues.why: This is the central reason the site reads as a template rather than an identity. The persistent blush-pink/cream/butter-yellow English-garden watercolors are romantic, but they belong to a different wedding than the one in your photos. Your proposal imagery is unmistakably Aegean — cobalt domes, whitewashed stone, deep blue sea. Warm rose-and-butter and cool cobalt are near-complementary; placing them on every page creates a low-grade visual dissonance the eye reads as 'this decoration didn't come with these pictures.' It also means your most ownable story element never touches the design system.
issues.fix: Two routes, in order of preference. (1) Switch to a Zola theme whose decorative palette and motifs lean cool/Mediterranean — blue-and-white, tile, stone, or a clean botanical in blues/greens — so the ornament finally rhymes with the photography. This is the cleanest fix but is a theme switch (flag: it will reset some layout, re-check after). (2) If you want to keep this theme, change the theme accent color away from sage toward a Cycladic blue drawn straight from the church domes, and lean on cool-toned hero/section photos so the cobalt in the images becomes the de-facto accent — that narrows the gap even while the warm florals remain. Honest limitation: the florals themselves are baked into the template art and usually can't be recolored or removed individually in Zola without changing themes.

issues.title: Sage-green is the single most-defaulted wedding accent of the decade — and here it bridges to the template, not to your story
issues.why: Sage (#505956) on white is tasteful, but it's also the safest, most-seen wedding color going, so it does nothing to make the identity feel like yours. Worse, strategically it ties the palette to the warm cottage florals rather than to the blue-and-white world your photos actually live in. You customized exactly one thing — the accent — and spent it agreeing with the stock art instead of with your own narrative.
issues.fix: Re-pull the accent from your own photography. Eyedrop the cobalt of the church dome or the deep Aegean blue and set that as the Zola theme color (Zola lets you set a custom theme/accent hex). It will read as intentional and personal the instant a guest who's seen the photos lands on the page — the color will literally be the color of the place he proposed. Keep the near-black body text as-is for contrast.

issues.title: Sage Cormorant at 400 weight, all-caps and tracked, is delicate on white — watch contrast and small sizes
issues.why: #505956 on #FFFFFF lands around a 5.2:1 contrast ratio — comfortably fine for large display type, but only borderline-comfortable for anything at normal text size, and Cormorant is a high-contrast face with thin strokes that an all-caps treatment plus 2px tracking makes even airier. Anywhere the sage Cormorant drops toward body size (sub-labels, smaller H-levels), legibility softens, especially on phones in sunlight. Separately, the live countdown in 'small light sans' is a hierarchy weak point — it's near-black so contrast is okay, but at small size it's easy to miss the one number guests scan for.
issues.fix: Keep sage Cormorant for genuinely large display only (wordmark, H1, the names). For any smaller heading or label, either bump the size or let it fall back to the near-black Circular rather than shrinking the thin sage serif. For the countdown, treat the number/date with more weight or size so it reads as a focal element, not fine print — it's the single most-scanned data point on the home page.

issues.title: The hero shows the payoff (the kiss) before guests know who, when, or where
issues.why: The no-text hero is a good instinct, but the three-panel band ends on the kiss and carries no names, date, or location — so a guest must scroll the full ~850px band before the Names Band confirms whose wedding this is and when. The ~40px wordmark sitting on white above an 850px image is also under-scaled to act as the masthead that anchors a text-free hero. The emotional climax arrives before the setup.
issues.fix: Reorder the three hero panels so the band reads setup → scene → reaction/kiss (left-to-right), letting the wider clifftop establishing shot lead and the kiss resolve at the right edge — Zola lets you reorder gallery/photo blocks. Then either add a slim text block immediately under the hero (names + 'June 18, 2027 · Whitehouse Station, NJ') before the long story, or accept the text-free hero but make the wordmark do more work. You can't enlarge the themed wordmark much, but you can make the Names Band sit higher/tighter to the hero so the who/when answers arrive faster.

issues.title: The registry grid undercuts the site's taste with curation, not design
issues.why: You don't control the grid's chrome, but you control the mix, and the mix breaks the spell. A $480 Le Creuset flagged 'Our most wanted' sharing a grid with a ~$13 O-Cedar spin mop reads as a list, not a curated table — and visually, utilitarian cleaning gear next to heirloom cookware flattens the elegance the rest of the site works to build. The honeymoon fund is also the strongest narrative item on the page (return to the place he proposed) and it currently has NO description at all.
issues.fix: Within Zola's curation controls: relocate purely utilitarian items (spin mop, basic storage) lower or trim them, and group the grid so the most beautiful objects — Le Creuset, Pillivuyt, the glassware — lead the eye. Keep the gold 'most wanted' star on a hero object. Most importantly, write a real description on the Honeymoon Fund tile that names the dream and ties to your story ('We got engaged on Sifnos — help us go back to the Cyclades'). It costs nothing, it's fully editable in Zola, and it converts the one cash tile from generic to personal.

issues.title: The section divider is a generic sprig — a missed chance for a tiny ownable mark
issues.why: The little floral sprig divider and the floral motif above the footer are template furniture. They're inoffensive, but in a site this typographically disciplined, generic ornament is the tell that everything decorative came from the box. A signature small mark is the cheapest way to make a template feel authored.
issues.fix: If you adopt a more Mediterranean theme, its native divider will likely already rhyme better. Staying put, the lowest-effort ownable move is to lean on a typographic device you already have — an ampersand or your 'M&O' set in Cormorant — as a repeating motif in your own added text/photo blocks, rather than introducing more florals. Zola won't let you upload a custom CSS divider, but you fully control the text blocks where a typographic mark can live.

conceptSeeds.title: Re-theme the whole site around the blue-and-white you already own
conceptSeeds.idea: Treat the Cyclades as the brand. Choose a Zola theme with a cool Mediterranean palette, set the accent to a cobalt eyedropped from the church dome, and let whitewashed-stone neutrals and Aegean blue replace the warm-garden florals. Keep the Cormorant/Circular type system exactly as-is — it will sit even better against blue-and-white. The result: the moment a guest sees the site, the color of the place Owen proposed is the color of the page. Identity and story finally become the same thing.

conceptSeeds.title: A typographic monogram as the connective tissue
conceptSeeds.idea: Build a tiny 'M & O' or ampersand mark in Cormorant and use it as your recurring device — section dividers, the footer motif, a watermark over a hero panel, the favicon-in-spirit. It replaces borrowed florals with a mark that is unambiguously yours, costs nothing, and reinforces the editorial masthead feeling the type is already reaching for. Fully buildable inside Zola text blocks.

conceptSeeds.title: Turn the honeymoon fund into the story's third act
conceptSeeds.idea: Right now the proposal story ends on the home page and the honeymoon fund is a blank tile. Connect them: a short 'Back to the Cyclades' moment — one of the blue-dome photos, a line of Cormorant, and a fund description that lets guests literally help send you back to Sifnos. It closes the narrative loop (met by butterfly effect → engaged on the island → returning together) and gives the single cash tile a reason to be the most-clicked thing on the page.

critiques.singleHighestPriority: Resolve the palette war between the warm cottage-garden florals and your cool Cycladic blue-white photography — ideally by switching to a Mediterranean/blue-white Zola theme (or, failing that, retiring sage and setting the accent to a cobalt pulled straight from the church domes). This one move is the lever that turns an elegant-but-default template into an identity that is recognizably about Meghan, Owen, and the island where he proposed. Everything else (type, story, hierarchy) is already good enough to shine once the decoration stops contradicting the photos.

critiques.persona: Marcus Webb, UX & Information Architecture Lead — navigation, user flows, mobile reflow, accessibility
critiques.overallImpression: From a pure IA standpoint, Meghan and Owen have a clean, well-organized skeleton: a centered wordmark, a single-row 7-item nav with a clear active-state underline, and generous whitespace that makes the structure easy to read. The two built pages are content-first in spirit — the home page tells a genuinely strong, specific story, and the registry is well-curated with a clear "most wanted" anchor. But the site has one structural gap that overrides almost everything else for a wedding site: there is no way for a guest to RSVP, and no way to find the date, location, or venue without scrolling past a wordless three-photo hero. A wedding website is a task tool first and a keepsake second. Right now it functions beautifully as a keepsake and incompletely as a task tool. With 13 months of runway, the fixes are very achievable inside Zola's limits — and most are about ordering, labeling, and surfacing information you already have, not about adding anything new. I'll be candid about the findability issues, but the bones here are good and the story is a real asset that the structure is currently under-using.
strengths: Navigation clarity: a single-row, 7-item horizontal nav with a clear underlined active state is exactly the right pattern. Seven items is at the comfortable upper edge but still scannable, the labels are mostly self-explanatory (Schedule, Travel, Registry, Gallery, FAQs), and the centered wordmark gives a stable 'home' affordance. This is a solid, conventional structure guests will navigate without instruction.

Content-first storytelling on Home: the OUR LOVE STORY section is genuinely well-written, specific, and ordered logically (how they met -> who they are together -> the proposal). The narrow ~60-70 character measure is a deliberate, correct typographic choice for reading comfort. This is the emotional core and it earns its place.

Registry findability is strong: the gold-star 'Our most wanted' flag on the Le Creuset Dutch Oven gives guests an immediate decision anchor, the Zola filter row (price, store, availability, category) lets people sort by budget, and the 'Requested: 1 / Still needs: 1' status prevents duplicate gifting. The price spread ($13–$480) means every guest can find something in their range — that's thoughtful inclusive design.

Restraint and whitespace: white header, no border, lots of breathing room. The absence of clutter means the few elements present carry clear hierarchy. Nothing competes for attention in the chrome, which is the right call.

A live countdown and a clearly stated date/location on the names band give the home page a real 'wedding hub' moment once you reach it — the right information is present, it's just positioned a scroll too low.

issues.title: No RSVP anywhere — the single most important guest task is missing from the nav
issues.why: RSVP is the primary job a wedding website does. There is no RSVP link in the 7-item nav and no RSVP entry point on the home page. A guest who lands here cannot complete the one action the couple most needs from them. Even at 13 months out this is a structural hole, not a 'later' item, because the nav is the site's contract with guests about what they can do here. Its absence also makes the site read as informational-only, training guests to expect a paper RSVP and ignore the site.
issues.fix: Zola has a native RSVP page — enable it and add 'RSVP' to the nav. Place it either first or last in the row so it reads as the primary call to action (first-position 'RSVP | Home | Schedule...' or a visually emphasized last item). Until you're collecting responses, the page can hold a short 'RSVPs open soon' note. This is fully within Zola and is the highest-leverage change on the site.

issues.title: Date, location, and venue are buried below a wordless hero — guests must scroll to learn the basics
issues.why: The hero is a full-bleed band of three engagement photos (~850px tall) with no names, no date, and no location overlaid. The first thing a guest sees is the kiss — beautiful, but it answers 'who is in love' rather than the practical 'who, when, where' a guest needs first. On a phone that ~850px hero can fill the entire first screen, pushing the date/location/countdown band a full swipe down. For a save-the-date link or a guest double-checking the date, the core facts should be reachable without scrolling.
issues.fix: Two Zola-feasible options. (1) Reorder: move the NAMES BAND (names + 'JUNE 18, 2027' + 'Whitehouse Station, NJ' + countdown) above or immediately interleaved with the hero so the facts appear in the first screen. (2) If your theme supports a hero text overlay or a header image with caption, place names + date + location on the hero itself. At minimum, ensure the date and city are visible without a scroll on mobile. The venue name (not just the town) should also appear here once known.

issues.title: Color contrast risk: sage-green Cormorant on pure white, plus a tiny light-sans countdown
issues.why: Sage-green #505956 on #FFFFFF computes to roughly a 6.4:1 contrast ratio — that actually passes WCAG AA for normal text, which is good news for the body-size uses. The real risks are: (a) Cormorant Garamond at weight 400 in all-caps with letter-spacing is a thin, low-ink typeface, so perceived legibility at small sizes is worse than the raw ratio suggests, especially for older guests (a wedding's most contrast-sensitive audience); and (b) the live countdown is described as 'small light sans' near-black but tiny — small type plus thin weight is where readability fails. All-caps also slows reading speed for longer strings.
issues.fix: Keep sage for large display headings and the wordmark where it's elegant and passes. Avoid sage for any small or secondary text; keep small UI text in the near-black #0E0E0E body color. For the countdown, increase the size and/or weight and label the units clearly ('Days / Hours / Min / Sec'). Reserve all-caps for short labels only — never for a full sentence. These are all reachable via Zola's font/color/size controls without a theme switch.

issues.title: Mobile reflow of the 3-image hero is likely to weaken the proposal moment
issues.why: Three side-by-side panels with no gutters read as a triptych on desktop, but on a narrow phone they will either (a) stack vertically — turning one wide cinematic moment into three tall stacked images that push everything below them far down the page, or (b) shrink so the wider center 'proposal scene' loses its subject. Either way the intended single-glance composition degrades on the device most guests will actually use, and it compounds the 'facts are buried' problem above.
issues.fix: Within Zola: consider making the hero a single strong image (the center proposal scene or the kiss) rather than a three-panel band, and move the other two photos into the existing stacked photo section lower down — they're already there in the body. A single hero reflows predictably on mobile and gets your facts higher on the screen. If you keep the triptych on desktop, at least confirm the mobile stack order leads with the strongest single frame and test it on a real phone.

issues.title: Honeymoon Fund has no context — guests can't tell what they're funding
issues.why: The cash-fund tile sits alone at the very bottom with 'Contribute what you wish' and no description of the honeymoon or destination. Cash funds convert far better when guests understand the story behind them, and given the couple's Greek-island proposal there's an obvious, charming narrative to tell. Position also matters: burying it at the bottom of a ~5350px page after 33 physical items means many guests never see it, and those who prefer to give an experience rather than a kitchen item have to hunt for the option.
issues.fix: Add a 1–2 sentence description to the honeymoon fund tile (Zola allows fund descriptions) — tie it to the proposal locale or wherever they're headed. Consider giving it more prominence: Zola lets you feature/reorder registry items, so surface the fund near the top alongside the 'most wanted' anchor, or add a short line in the registry intro pointing guests to it. This is curation/copy only, fully within Zola.

issues.title: Registry is heavily kitchen/home-skewed, which narrows guest choice
issues.why: Of ~33 items, the inventory skews almost entirely to cookware, dinnerware, glassware, and small appliances — down to an O-Cedar spin mop. That's a coherent, practical registry, but from a 'can every guest find a fitting gift' standpoint it's narrow: guests who want to give something more personal, experiential, or celebratory have only the single (currently context-free) honeymoon fund as an alternative. Variety also helps guests at different price points and relationships to the couple feel they're giving something meaningful rather than checking a utilitarian box.
issues.fix: Optional and entirely the couple's call — but consider adding a few non-kitchen categories Zola supports (an experience/date-night fund, a 'first home' or hosting item or two, a charitable option if it fits them). Even 3–5 items outside the kitchen broadens the emotional range without diluting the practical core. The mop is fine and honestly endearing; just balance it.

issues.title: Nav label and ordering could better match guest priority and the active-state needs verifying
issues.why: The nav order (Home | Schedule | Travel | Registry | Wedding Party | Gallery | FAQs) is reasonable, but once RSVP is added the row becomes 8 items, which is past the comfortable single-row limit and risks wrapping or crowding on smaller screens. Also, the only wayfinding signal is an underline on the active label; underline-only active states can be subtle, and you'll want to confirm it's distinguishable enough (it is the sole indicator of 'where am I'). 'Schedule' vs the more common 'Events' is a minor label choice worth a sanity check against guest expectations.
issues.fix: When you add RSVP, pressure-test the row on mobile — Zola typically collapses to a hamburger menu on small screens, so confirm the order there leads with RSVP and Home. Consider whether all 8 items need top-level placement or whether something like Gallery could live lower once content exists. Keep the underlined active state but verify its visibility; if Zola offers a bolder active treatment in your theme, use it. Label choices are pure copy and free to adjust.

conceptSeeds.title: Make the Greek-island proposal the site's spine, not just a photo set
conceptSeeds.idea: Right now the Cycladic blue-white-stone story lives only in the hero photos and the love-story text, while the visual system is generic English-cottage watercolor florals — a real tension. As content gets built out, let the proposal location quietly thread through the site as a wayfinding and theming device: a single recurring motif (e.g., a Mediterranean blue accent or a thin horizon-line divider) tying the home story, the honeymoon fund description, and eventually the gallery together. This is a structure/coherence idea — one ownable thread that makes the site feel like theirs rather than the template's, and gives guests a consistent visual cue across pages. If the watercolor florals can't be removed in this theme, flag it: a theme switch may be the only way to fully resolve the style mismatch, so weigh that against the effort.

conceptSeeds.title: A 'Start here' guest path on the home page
conceptSeeds.idea: Once the placeholder pages fill in, add a short, scannable 'Here's what you need to know' strip near the top of Home — date, location, a one-line 'RSVP by [date]' with a button, and quick links to Travel and Schedule. Wedding guests arrive with three questions (when, where, what do I do) and a deliberate at-a-glance answer block respects their time and dramatically improves findability versus making them hunt through the nav. This is the IA equivalent of a lobby directory and is buildable with Zola text + button blocks.

conceptSeeds.title: Design the FAQ as the site's findability safety net
conceptSeeds.idea: When you build FAQs, treat it as the catch-all that absorbs every question the nav can't answer in one tap — dress code, kids, parking, the Friday-wedding logistics, plus-ones, timing. A well-structured FAQ reduces the load on every other page and on the couple's inbox. Group questions by theme and lead with the highest-frequency ones. It's the single highest-ROI page for reducing guest confusion, and it's pure content within Zola.

critiques.singleHighestPriority: Enable Zola's native RSVP page and add 'RSVP' to the navigation (first or last position, visually emphasized). It's the one task a wedding website must support, it's currently entirely absent, and it's trivially fixable within Zola today — no theme switch, no custom code. Pair it with surfacing the date and location above the fold on Home so a guest can answer 'when, where, and how do I respond' without hunting.

critiques.persona: Eleanor Hartley, Luxury Wedding Planner — Guest Experience & Etiquette
critiques.overallImpression: There is a real wedding here with a genuinely lovely story, and that is the hardest part to fake — you already have it. The proposal narrative (the 36-hour secret flight, Sophia's mother touring Owen around the island all day, "the easiest yes of all time") is specific, warm, and earns the reader's affection. The love-story copy is the strongest element on the entire site and reads like real people, not template filler. But I have to judge this the way a guest will experience it, and a guest does not arrive to be charmed — they arrive with questions, and a good website answers them before they have to ask. On the two built pages, the site is currently composed as a romantic keepsake for the couple rather than as a working tool for guests. The most consequential fact about this wedding — that it falls on a FRIDAY — appears nowhere with any emphasis, and a Friday date reshapes every guest's travel, lodging, and time-off decisions. The hero leads with a kiss and no names, date, or place, so a guest must scroll to learn the most basic facts. There is no RSVP path in the navigation, which is the one action you most need from every household. And the registry, while sweetly practical, ends on a "Honeymoon Fund" tile with no honeymoon attached to it. None of this is fatal — you are thirteen months out, which is a luxury of time — but the built pages currently prioritize sentiment over service, and weddings live or die on service. The good news: nearly every fix I'm recommending is achievable inside Zola's settings, copy fields, and image choices. You don't need to fight the platform. You need to make the built pages do a little more hosting.
strengths: The love-story copy is exceptional — specific, funny, and human. 'One week into dating, Meghan told her friend Sophia, I am going to marry him. Five years later, here we are' and 'the easiest yes of all time' are the kind of lines that make guests genuinely happy to be invited. Most couples write nothing this good; do not let anyone sand the personality out of it.

The restraint of the type system is tasteful. Cormorant Garamond in sage on white, all-caps with generous letter-spacing, is a quiet, grown-up choice that reads as elegant rather than trendy. The wordmark and H1 treatment feel considered.
Generous whitespace and a clean, centered single-row nav give the site an unfussy, confident calm. Nothing is shouting, which is the right instinct for a wedding.

The engagement photography itself is a strong asset — a real proposal in a real, beautiful place. The raw material for a memorable visual identity is already in hand; it simply isn't being deployed to its full effect yet.

On the registry, the curation instinct is right: a clearly flagged 'Our most wanted' Le Creuset, a sensible spread of genuinely useful items, and a real price range so every guest can find something. Practical and unpretentious — guests appreciate being able to actually buy a gift rather than facing only $400 ceramics.

issues.title: No RSVP path exists anywhere in the navigation
issues.why: From a guest's standpoint this is the single most important function of a wedding website, and the one action the couple most needs in return. A guest who wants to respond — or who has misplaced a paper card — will land here, scan the nav (Home, Schedule, Travel, Registry, Wedding Party, Gallery, FAQs), find no way to reply, and email or text the couple instead. That converts a self-service action into manual inbound work for Meghan and Owen, and it risks responses being lost in DMs. Etiquette-wise, the absence also subtly signals 'not ready,' even though everything else is on track.
issues.fix: Enable Zola's RSVP page and add 'RSVP' to the nav — ideally as the first or last item so it reads as the primary call to action. Even thirteen months out, you can publish it now in a 'collecting addresses / save the date' mode, or keep it built but unlisted until invitations go out. The point is that the function exists and the guest is never left without a path. This is fully native to Zola and requires no theme change.

issues.title: The Friday wedding date is not flagged or contextualized
issues.why: June 18, 2027 is a Friday, and that is a load-bearing fact for every guest. A Friday wedding means time off work, earlier travel, a different childcare calculus, and for a rural New Jersey location, likely a Thursday-night or Friday-morning arrival. Right now the date renders as 'JUNE 18, 2027' with no day-of-week and no framing, so a meaningful share of guests will not register that it's a weekday until much later — exactly when it becomes expensive and stressful to adjust. Surfacing this early is pure hospitality; it is the difference between guests feeling guided and guests feeling ambushed.
issues.fix: On the home names band, render the date as 'Friday, June 18, 2027' rather than 'June 18, 2027' — a one-word copy change with outsized impact. Then add a single warm sentence below it or in the story area, something like 'We're getting married on a Friday evening — we'd love for you to make a weekend of it in New Jersey.' This reframes the weekday as an invitation rather than an inconvenience, and it pre-answers the question every guest is about to ask. Pure copy; no platform limitation.

issues.title: The hero withholds who, when, and where
issues.why: The three side-by-side engagement images are beautiful, but the hero leads with the kiss and carries no name, date, or location overlay, with the wordmark and nav sitting on white above the band. A first-time guest — especially an older relative or a plus-one who has never met the couple — must scroll past an 850px image band before learning whose wedding this is, when, and where. A wedding hero's first job is orientation: name, date, place, in that order, visible without scrolling. The emotional payoff (the kiss, the cliff) is wonderful, but it should reward a guest who already knows the facts, not stand in for them.
issues.fix: Two Zola-feasible options. Simplest: keep the photo band but add a slim text block immediately beneath it — and tighten it — so 'MEGHAN & OWEN · FRIDAY, JUNE 18, 2027 · WHITEHOUSE STATION, NJ' is visible almost immediately on load rather than after a full names-band scroll. Better, if the theme's hero block supports a single full-width image with an overlay caption: lead with the wider proposal scene (the cliff and the blue-domed church) as one hero image and let the kiss live in the gallery, with name/date/place set over or just under it. The aim is that the three orientation facts greet the guest at the top of the page, not midway down it.

issues.title: The built pages convey no practical hospitality for a rural NJ location
issues.why: I am deliberately not critiquing the unfinished Travel or Schedule pages. But on the BUILT pages, a guest gets zero orientation to the practical shape of this wedding, and Whitehouse Station is not a place most guests will know. It's rural Hunterdon County — not near a major airport hub or an obvious hotel cluster — which means out-of-town guests have real questions about flying in, driving, and where to sleep, and a Friday date sharpens all of them. With Home and Registry as the only live pages, there is currently nothing anywhere on the site that says 'here is roughly how this weekend will work,' so every logistics question routes straight to the couple. This is the etiquette heart of the matter: the site should reduce inbound questions, and right now it can't.
issues.fix: Add one short 'Getting Here' or 'The Weekend at a Glance' paragraph to the home page now — a placeholder-quality teaser is fine and is not the same as critiquing an empty page. Even three sentences ('We're marrying near Whitehouse Station in the New Jersey countryside, about [X] from Newark airport. We'll share hotel blocks and a weekend timeline here soon — for now, save Friday evening, June 18.') gives guests the mental model they need to start planning and signals that logistics are handled. This is a content addition to a built page, fully within Zola.

issues.title: The Honeymoon Fund has no honeymoon attached to it
issues.why: A cash fund works on exactly one thing: emotional permission. Guests give generously to a honeymoon they can picture and stingily, or not at all, to an unexplained money tile. The current 'Honeymoon Fund!' sits at the very bottom with no destination, no story, and no sense of what it funds — so it reads as a generic catch-all rather than as a gift toward something real. Given that this couple's whole love story is a Greek island, this is a remarkable missed opportunity; the honeymoon is the most evocative thing they could be funding and it's described as nothing.
issues.fix: Use Zola's fund description field to give it a destination and a sentence of story — for example, 'After getting engaged on a Greek island, we're dreaming of [a return to the Cyclades / a trip to somewhere new]. Your gift helps us toast to married life — a sunset dinner, a long boat day, the things we'll remember forever.' You can also break a single fund into two or three concrete experience tiles (Zola supports this), which consistently outperforms one vague pool. Curation-side only; fully within your control.

issues.title: A live seconds-ticking countdown undercuts the elegance and ages quickly
issues.why: The countdown rendered as '383 days 14 hours 24 minutes 33 seconds' in small light sans pulls the eye away from the date and reads more like an event-ticketing widget than a wedding. At thirteen months out, a second-by-second clock is also faintly comic — guests don't need to watch seconds tick toward a date over a year away — and the small light-sans treatment is the weakest typographic moment on an otherwise restrained page. It slightly cheapens the sage-and-Cormorant mood you've built everywhere else.
issues.fix: If the theme allows toggling countdown granularity or hiding it, prefer a simple 'Friday, June 18, 2027' with no live timer, or a quiet 'days to go' only. If the countdown can't be removed, at minimum ensure the date line above it is visually dominant — larger, in Cormorant sage — so the timer is a footnote rather than the headline. Minor, and theme-dependent; flag it as a polish item, not a priority.

issues.title: Contrast and legibility of sage-on-white and small light sans need a real check for older guests
issues.why: Sage-green Cormorant (#505956) on pure white is elegant, and the body sans at near-black is fine. But two things warrant scrutiny through a guest-accessibility lens: the all-caps Cormorant headings with wide letter-spacing are decorative and harder to read at a glance for older relatives, and the countdown described as 'small light sans' is the kind of low-contrast micro-type that fails for anyone over fifty — which at a wedding is a large and important share of the audience. Hospitality includes legibility; a grandmother should never have to squint to find the date.
issues.fix: Keep the all-caps Cormorant for the wordmark and H1s where it shines, but make sure the actual date, location, and any logistics text live in the readable body sans at a comfortable size and full near-black weight, not a light gray. Where Zola's font/color settings allow, nudge any 'light' weight UI text up to regular. The sage accent is lovely on headings; just don't let critical guest information inherit a decorative or low-contrast treatment.

issues.title: The English-cottage-garden florals tell a different story than the couple's Mediterranean proposal
issues.why: This is an aesthetic-coherence note, but it bears on guest experience because the visual system is the first impression and currently it's borrowed, not personal. The persistent blush-and-butter watercolor garden spray is generic to the template and sits in quiet conflict with the crisp blue-white-stone Cycladic photography that is the couple's actual, ownable story. The result is a site that looks like a pretty default rather than like Meghan and Owen specifically — and the single most distinctive thing about them (a Greek-island engagement) is doing none of the visual work.
issues.fix: Within Zola you likely can't swap individual floral illustrations, so this is partly a theme-selection question — flag honestly that fully resolving it may require choosing a cleaner or more Mediterranean theme. But you can move the needle without a theme change: lean the theme color toward a sea-blue or keep sage but pair it with more of the blue-white proposal imagery throughout, so the photography (which is personal) carries the identity and the florals recede. If a theme with lighter or no fixed florals is available, auditioning it is worth an afternoon. Be clear-eyed that the florals themselves may not be editable.

issues.title: The O-Cedar spin mop and a few utilitarian items read slightly off on a gift registry
issues.why: This is gentle etiquette, not a rule. A wedding registry is a list of things guests give as a celebration — and asking a guest to gift a $13 floor mop, however genuinely useful, can feel less like a present and more like a household errand. It won't offend anyone, but among Le Creuset and Pillivuyt it's a small tonal blip, and the steep number of strictly functional low-cost items can make the list read more 'new-apartment checklist' than 'help us celebrate.'
issues.fix: Optional and entirely the couple's call. If they want to keep budget-friendly entry points (which is thoughtful — not every guest can spend $80+), I'd swap the most chore-like items for charming-but-affordable ones: nice linen napkins, a good pepper mill, a bar tool, a cookbook the couple loves, a candle. Same price band, warmer to give. Pure curation; no platform constraint.

conceptSeeds.title: Let the Greek proposal become the site's spine, not just a photo
conceptSeeds.idea: The strongest, most ownable thing this couple has is the Sifnos engagement, and right now it lives only in two photo placements and one paragraph. Imagine the whole site carrying a soft thread of it: a sea-blue accent in the theme color, the blue-domed church as a recurring quiet motif, the FAQ and Travel pages (when built) written in the couple's warm, funny voice that the love story already proves they have. A future Gallery could open with a small 'How he pulled it off' photo sequence — Owen's 36-hour journey, Sophia's mom as co-conspirator. Guests would arrive already in on the romance. This turns a pretty template into unmistakably Meghan-and-Owen.

conceptSeeds.title: A 'Know Before You Go' card for a Friday countryside wedding
conceptSeeds.idea: Because this is a Friday wedding in rural New Jersey, a single, warmly written orientation block — eventually its own page, but it can start as a home-page paragraph — would do enormous hospitality work: the day-of-week framing, nearest airport and rough drive time, whether to plan a Thursday or Friday arrival, and a one-line 'make a weekend of it' invitation. It pre-answers the four questions every guest is about to text and sets a tone of being well looked-after. The couple's voice is already proven charming in the love story; pointing that same voice at logistics would make even the practical pages feel like hospitality rather than housekeeping.

conceptSeeds.title: Reframe the cash gift as 'fund the honeymoon we'll never forget'
conceptSeeds.idea: Given the engagement happened on a Greek island, the honeymoon fund is sitting on a goldmine of emotional resonance it isn't using. A future version could turn one anonymous fund into a few evocative, story-linked experience tiles — 'a sunset dinner on the water,' 'a day on a boat in the Cyclades,' 'our first married toast' — each with a sentence tying back to where it all began. Guests give to stories, and this couple has a genuinely good one. Done well, this both raises the warmth of the registry and quietly closes the narrative loop the love story opens.

critiques.singleHighestPriority: Make the built pages answer a guest's first questions before they have to ask — concretely, this means three tightly linked changes, with RSVP as the keystone: (1) enable and add an RSVP page to the navigation so the one action you most need from guests actually has a path; (2) surface who/when/where at the very top — render the date as 'Friday, June 18, 2027' and get name + date + location visible at or near the top of the home page rather than after a long scroll past the kiss; and (3) add one short, warm 'here's roughly how the weekend works' paragraph to a built page so guests aren't left guessing about a Friday wedding in rural New Jersey. Every piece of this is achievable inside Zola today, requires no theme change, and together it converts a beautiful keepsake into a website that actually hosts your guests. If you do only one thing this week, add the RSVP.

critiques.persona: Devin Carter — Product / Conversion Designer (jobs-to-be-done, CTAs, registry, RSVP funnels)
critiques.overallImpression: As a product, this site has a beautiful front door and no working doorbell. The craft is real: the love-story copy is specific, warm, and genuinely well-written (the "ultimate butterfly effect" roommate setup, "easiest yes of all time," the 36-hour secret flight) and the engagement photography is a strong, ownable asset. But judged by what a guest is actually here to DO, the funnel is broken at the most important step. A wedding website has a small set of primary jobs: figure out IF/WHEN/WHERE, decide to come, RSVP, get logistics (travel, schedule), and buy a gift. Right now only one of those jobs — gift-buying — has a real path, and even that path buries its highest-converting asset (the honeymoon fund) at the very bottom with zero framing. The single most damaging thing is that there is no RSVP entry point anywhere in the nav. RSVP is the conversion event of the entire site; it is the one action whose completion the couple is paying (in stress and follow-up texts) to collect. A guest who lands here cannot find the one button that matters. Everything else — the countdown ticking against a date no one can act on yet, the kiss-first hero with no names/date, the kitchen-heavy registry — are real conversion frictions stacked on top of that hole. The good news: nearly every fix is copy, ordering, and Zola page-toggle work the couple can do this weekend. 13 months of runway means timing is on their side; the structure is not yet.
strengths: The love-story copy is the best converting asset on the site and it's genuinely excellent. It's specific (not Mad-Libs wedding boilerplate), it has narrative shape (meet-cute -> 'I'm going to marry him' one week in -> surprise proposal payoff), and the kicker lines land ('the easiest yes of all time'). This is the emotional fuel that makes guests want to show up and spend money — it's doing real work.

The engagement photography is a distinctive, premium asset. A blue-domed Cycladic clifftop proposal is instantly recognizable and ownable — far better than the usual field-at-golden-hour set. As raw conversion material (trust, aspiration, 'these people throw a beautiful event'), it's strong.

The registry has a clear, healthy primary path per item: image, price, and a single unambiguous 'Buy on [Store]' button with live 'Still needs: 1' scarcity. That card pattern is a clean, well-understood purchase action — Zola's templating is actually helping here.

Smart use of the 'Our most wanted' gold-star flag on the Le Creuset Dutch Oven. That's exactly the right merchandising move — it gives decisive gift-givers an anchor and reduces choice paralysis. Good instinct, just under-deployed (only one item carries it).

A genuinely useful price spread (~$13 to $480) means every guest budget has a landing spot. From a conversion standpoint, having sub-$30 items AND a $480 hero item is correct — nobody is priced out, and aspirational givers have somewhere to go.

Restraint on the hero: no cheesy overlaid script text, letting the photos breathe. The instinct toward elegance and whitespace is sound — the problem is what's missing (names/date/CTA), not visual clutter.

issues.title: No RSVP entry point anywhere — the site's single most important conversion action does not exist
issues.area: Information architecture / primary CTA / RSVP funnel
issues.why: RSVP is THE conversion event of a wedding site. It's the one action the couple actually needs collected to run their event — catering counts, seating, vendor numbers all depend on it. The nav (Home | Schedule | Travel | Registry | Wedding Party | Gallery | FAQs) has no RSVP, and there is no primary CTA button anywhere on the home page. A guest who wants to say 'yes, I'm coming' has nowhere to click. Every other charming thing on this site is in service of this action, and the action is missing. Note: it's reasonable that RSVP isn't OPEN yet at 13 months out — but the slot, and a 'RSVP opens [month]' placeholder, should exist so the IA and guest expectation are set now.
issues.fix: In Zola, enable the RSVP page and add it to the nav as a distinct, rightmost item (RSVP reads as the terminal action). Until invitations go out, set its content to a dated promise: 'RSVPs will open in early 2027 — please hold [date range].' Separately, add a single primary CTA on the home page directly under the countdown — a button styled in the sage accent linking to RSVP (or, pre-open, to Schedule/Travel). One clear next action per screen is the core principle being violated.

issues.title: Honeymoon fund is buried at the very bottom with zero framing — the highest-emotion gift option is hidden and unsold
issues.why: After the love story you just told — a couple whose defining moment was a Greek island — the honeymoon fund is the single most on-brand, highest-emotional-resonance gift you can offer. Instead it sits dead last beneath ~33 kitchen items, labeled only 'Honeymoon Fund!' with 'Contribute what you wish' and NO description of where they're going or why. Cash funds convert on story and specificity ('help us snorkel in X', 'a dinner on the cliffs'). A naked, undescribed fund at position 34 will be skipped by exactly the guests most moved by the proposal story. This is leaving the easiest, most meaningful conversions on the table.
issues.fix: All Zola-feasible: (1) Reorder so the honeymoon fund appears near the TOP of the registry, ideally pinned right after the 'most wanted' anchor. (2) Write a real 2-3 sentence description tied to the story ('After getting engaged in the Greek islands, we're dreaming up our next adventure — your gift helps fund the honeymoon, from the flights to that one ridiculous cliffside dinner'). (3) Consider breaking the single fund into 2-3 named tiles ('A night out in [place],' 'A day on the water') — itemized funds convert better than one open-ended ask because they feel concrete. (4) Add a one-line registry intro at the top pointing guests to it.

issues.title: Hero shows the kiss with no names, no date, no location — guests must scroll ~900px to learn the most basic facts
issues.why: The #1 job for a first-time visitor is the fastest possible answer to who is getting married, when, and where — that's the decision-to-attend trigger and what people screenshot to add to their calendar. The current hero is a beautiful three-panel kiss with zero text; the names/date/'Whitehouse Station, NJ' don't appear until the names band below the fold. On mobile especially, the answer to 'should I save June 18, 2027?' is pushed off-screen behind a wordless image. We're spending the most valuable real estate on emotion and deferring the function.
issues.fix: Within Zola's limits: choose a hero/header image that supports an overlaid title, or use the theme's hero text option to place 'MEGHAN & OWEN — JUNE 18, 2027 — WHITEHOUSE STATION, NJ' over the imagery. If this theme can't overlay text on the hero, move the names band tighter to the top so date + location clear the fold immediately, and reduce the hero band height (~850px is very tall) so the critical facts are visible without a deliberate scroll. The kiss can stay — just don't make it the only thing above the fold.

issues.title: The live countdown is high-visibility real estate spent on a low-value action (and it's hard to read)
issues.why: A second-resolution countdown ('383 days 14 hours 24 minutes 33 seconds') 13 months out creates urgency toward an action the guest cannot take (RSVP isn't open, travel isn't bookable yet). It's motion and prominence with no conversion payoff — and seconds ticking can read as gimmicky on a wedding site. Worse, it's set in small light sans, so the one genuinely useful number (the date itself) competes with a noisy timer. We're drawing the eye to the least actionable element on the page.
issues.fix: Two Zola-feasible options: (a) keep the countdown but simplify it to days only and demote it below a primary CTA, so the hierarchy is Date/Place -> Action -> (then a light flourish countdown); or (b) replace it with a single calm line ('Friday, June 18, 2027 · Whitehouse Station, NJ') and an 'Add to calendar'/Save-the-date affordance. Reserve urgency mechanics for when there's something to be urgent about (RSVP deadline window in 2027). Also bump the date's type size/weight so it's the clear anchor of the band.

issues.title: Registry inventory skews almost entirely kitchen/home — thin coverage for non-cookware gift-givers, and one off-tone item
issues.why: Of ~33 items, the overwhelming majority are cookware, dinnerware, glassware, small appliances, and storage. That's a coherent registry, but conversion-wise it under-serves givers who don't want to gift another sauté pan and want experiences, home/lifestyle, or 'something they'll remember us by.' A near-monoculture also makes the page feel long and same-y (~5350px) and pushes the honeymoon fund even further down. The O-Cedar spin mop, while practical, is an odd emotional note on a registry attached to a Greek-island engagement — it can cheapen the set for a browsing guest.
issues.fix: Curation is fully in the couple's control. Broaden into 3-4 non-kitchen lanes Zola supports: experience/cash funds (date-night fund, a 'first adventure' fund), a few higher-touch home pieces (the West Elm duvet shows the instinct — add a couple more), and 1-2 keepsake/hosting items. Either remove the spin mop or tuck purely utilitarian basics into a single bottom group so the top of the registry leads with desirable, on-brand pieces. Add 2-3 more 'most wanted' stars at varied price points to anchor decisions across budgets.

issues.title: Decorative system fights the brand the photos establish — generic English-garden florals vs. crisp Cycladic blue-white
issues.why: From a product-brand standpoint, the strongest equity this couple owns is the Greek-island story and its blue-domed, white-stone, Mediterranean look. The persistent watercolor English-cottage florals (blush/butter/sage) are generic template furniture pulling in a completely different direction — soft romantic-garden vs. crisp sun-and-sea. The mismatch dilutes the one thing that makes the site theirs and makes the design read as 'stock Zola theme' rather than 'Meghan & Owen.' Brand coherence is a conversion factor: a site that feels personally theirs earns more trust and engagement than one that feels templated.
issues.fix: Realistic about Zola: you can't hand-place custom illustrations, so this is partly a theme decision — flag it honestly. Lowest-effort win: keep this theme but lean the sage accent and let PHOTOGRAPHY carry the identity (more Sifnos blue-white imagery in galleries, section breaks, and as the home hero). Bigger move (theme switch, platform stretch): if Zola offers a cleaner, more minimal/Mediterranean theme with less floral weight, switching would let the blue-white palette and crisp photos define the brand. At minimum, reduce reliance on the floral motifs as the primary identity and make the proposal imagery the recurring visual signature.

issues.title: Sage Cormorant on pure white + small light sans countdown — likely contrast/legibility shortfalls, especially mobile
issues.why: Sage-green (#505956) headings on #FFFFFF is elegant but borderline for low-vision and bright-sunlight mobile reading; thin Cormorant 400 at large all-caps with 2px tracking trades legibility for style, and all-caps slows reading of anything beyond short labels. The countdown in 'small light sans' is the likeliest hard-fail. Accessibility is a conversion issue: guests skew older (parents, grandparents) and a meaningful share will be reading on phones outdoors. If they can't comfortably read the date or find the action, they bounce to a text-the-couple workaround — defeating the site's purpose.
issues.fix: Within Zola's font/color controls: keep sage for large display headings but ensure body and UI text use the near-black #0E0E0E (the dossier says it does — good). Darken or weight-up the countdown text so it isn't light-gray-small. Avoid setting paragraph-length copy in all-caps Cormorant; reserve all-caps for short titles only (the love-story body is already sans — keep it that way). If the theme allows a slightly darker sage for headings, nudge it to clear WCAG AA on white.

issues.title: Home page has no exit ramp to the rest of the site — the story ends, then dumps into the footer
issues.area: Home page / internal navigation / cross-page flow
issues.why: After the proposal photos, the home page drops straight to the Zola footer with no in-content next step. A guest who's now emotionally bought-in ('great, I'm in — what do I do?') has to go back up to the top nav to continue. In conversion terms, the warmest moment — right after the story payoff — is exactly where you should hand them their next action, and instead the path goes cold.
issues.fix: Add a simple closing content block under the proposal photos with 1-3 inline links/buttons to the next jobs: 'RSVP' (or 'RSVPs open soon'), 'Plan your trip' (Travel), 'See the registry.' Zola text/button blocks handle this. Capture intent at peak emotion instead of relying on the user to scroll back to the header.

conceptSeeds.title: Make the honeymoon a destination, not a footnote — a 'Greek-island continued' fund experience
conceptSeeds.idea: The proposal story basically pre-sells a Mediterranean honeymoon. Lean all the way in: a short Registry-top section (or a dedicated page when ready) framed as 'Where we're headed next,' with 1-2 blue-white photos and 2-3 named contribution tiles ('A sunset dinner on the cliffs,' 'A day on the water,' 'The flights'). Itemized, story-driven, photo-backed cash funds convert dramatically better than one undescribed 'Honeymoon Fund!' tile — and this is the single biggest untapped emotional-to-action conversion on the site.

conceptSeeds.title: An 'At a glance' guest card on the home page — the 5-second job, solved
conceptSeeds.idea: Most visits are quick fact-checks. Add a compact info block high on the home page (Zola text/columns) answering the top jobs in one glance: Date (Fri Jun 18, 2027) · Place (Whitehouse Station, NJ) · 'RSVP by [date]' · 'Dress code TBD' · a calendar/save affordance. It turns a scroll-to-find experience into a screenshot-and-go one, which is exactly what out-of-town guests do on their phones.

conceptSeeds.title: Let the photography become the brand system as pages get built
conceptSeeds.idea: Rather than fighting the template florals, plan the (in-scope, future) visual identity around the Sifnos blue-white palette and the engagement set: use crisp proposal/island imagery as section dividers and hero imagery, pull the accent toward the sea-and-stone story, and let real photos — not generic watercolor — be the recurring motif. This makes the whole site unmistakably theirs and raises trust/engagement across every job, all within Zola's photo/gallery blocks.

critiques.singleHighestPriority: Create the RSVP entry point. Add an RSVP slot to the nav (rightmost) and a single primary CTA on the home page — even pre-open as 'RSVPs open in early 2027,' so the funnel exists and guests know where the one action that matters will live. RSVP is the conversion event of the entire site, and right now there is no door to it.

critiques.persona: Julia Fontaine — Brand Storyteller (voice, narrative & microcopy)
critiques.overallImpression: As a writer, this is the most pleasant surprise on the site: the Our Love Story copy is genuinely good. It's specific, it's earned, and it has at least two lines I'd fight to keep — "One week into dating, Meghan told her friend Sophia, 'I am going to marry him.' Five years later, here we are," and "It was the easiest yes of all time." Those are real, ownable, this-couple sentences, not Pinterest filler. The proposal arc (36 hours of secret travel, a whole island in on it, sunset on the second-to-last night) is a gift of a story and it's mostly told well. So my critique is not "fix the writing" — it's "the writing is your strongest asset and right now it's underdeployed." The voice that's so alive in paragraph three vanishes everywhere else: the wordmark, the section heading, the honeymoon fund, the date/place lines, the footer. Meghan's type-A wit and Owen's calm romance are *named* in paragraph two but rarely *performed* anywhere on the page. The result is a site where one beautiful story is quarantined in a single block of text while the rest speaks in default Zola. There's also a small but real craft issue: a few possessives appear to be missing apostrophes ("Meghan roommate," "Sophia mom"), and paragraph one is carrying one very long, breath-holding sentence. Easy fixes, all of them, and all within Zola's text blocks. Tighten the prose a touch, then let that voice leak — deliberately, sparingly — into the microcopy so the whole site sounds like them and not like the template.
strengths: The love story is specific and emotionally precise — it names a real chain of events (roommates dating, the 'butterfly effect' of almost not living together) rather than reaching for clichés. Specificity is the whole game in this genre, and they've already won it.

Two lines are pure gold and instantly ownable: 'I am going to marry him. Five years later, here we are' and 'It was the easiest yes of all time.' These are the kinds of sentences guests quote back to the couple. Build the voice outward from these.

Paragraph two does the rare, generous thing of characterizing BOTH people distinctly — 'hilarious, organized, and unapologetically type A' vs. 'calm, go-with-the-flow, endlessly romantic, and just as funny.' Most couples write a mushy 'we' blur; they actually drew two portraits.

The proposal paragraph has genuine narrative shape — secrecy, stakes (36 hours), a supporting cast (Sophia's mom touring Owen around all day), and a payoff at sunset. It reads like a told story, with a beginning, middle, and a landing.
The emotional thesis is clear and not overwrought: the most special part wasn't the proposal itself but how many people conspired in the joy of it. That's a mature, warm note to end on.

issues.title: The couple's voice lives in ONE block of text and nowhere else on the site
issues.why: Paragraph three proves Meghan & Owen can write with warmth and wit — but the moment you leave the Our Love Story section, the site reverts to default Zola voice: a generic 'Registry' H1, an undescribed honeymoon fund, Zola's own footer line ('For all the days along the way'), and no voiced welcome anywhere up top. A guest meets the couple's personality only if they scroll into one paragraph. The brand sounds like them for ~150 words and like a template for the other ~8,000px. That's the single biggest storytelling miss, and it's invisible because the one piece that exists is good.
issues.fix: Treat voice as a system, not a paragraph. In Zola text blocks you fully control, add 3-4 short voiced touches that echo the love-story tone: (1) a one-line welcome in the names band or just under the hero — e.g. 'We're getting married — and somehow we talked an entire Greek island into helping ask.' (2) Rename/subtitle the registry page with a line of their own (see registry issue). (3) Replace nothing you can't, but ADD a voiced line above Zola's footer if the theme allows a closing text block. The rule: every place a guest's eye lands should sound like the people who wrote paragraph three.

issues.title: The honeymoon fund has zero story — and it's the most on-brand item they have
issues.why: This couple's entire identity is a Greek-island proposal, and the honeymoon fund tile is the one registry item that could carry that story — yet it reads as bare Zola default: 'Contribute what you wish / Contribute to gift / This is a Group Gift,' with NO description of the honeymoon or destination. It's the emotional climax of the registry buried as a generic afterthought at the very bottom. Guests give more, and more happily, when a cash fund tells them what they're part of. Right now it asks for money with no narrative and no warmth.
issues.fix: Write a 2-3 sentence description on the fund (Zola lets you add fund text). Lean into the Greece thread: e.g. 'We got engaged on a clifftop in the Cyclades at sunset — and we're not done with the Greek islands yet. A contribution here helps send us back to the Aegean as a married couple (and yes, we'll find that little blue-domed church again).' Give it a title with personality instead of 'Honeymoon Fund!' — something like 'Send Us Back to the Aegean' or 'Our Greek Encore.' This is the highest-leverage 10 minutes of copywriting on the whole site.

issues.title: A few possessives are missing apostrophes — a type-A bride will wince
issues.why: In the proposal paragraph the dossier shows 'Meghan roommate who introduced them,' 'Meghan friends,' and 'Sophia mom.' If those render literally on the live page, they're plain grammar errors in the site's most-read, most-emotional passage — and they sit oddly next to a paragraph that proudly calls Meghan 'organized and unapologetically type A.' Errors in the love story undercut the care the rest of the writing shows; it's the one place guests read slowly.
issues.fix: Quick proofread pass in the text block: 'Meghan's roommate,' 'Meghan's friends,' 'Sophia's mom.' While in there, confirm 'Edie' and 'Sophia' are spelled consistently. Two-minute fix, outsized credibility payoff.

issues.title: Paragraph one buries its best beat under a long, breath-holding sentence
issues.why: The meet-cute is great but the second sentence runs ~45 words ('In fact, Meghan did not really know her roommate before moving in and almost did not live with her at all, which makes the fact that they met feel like the ultimate butterfly effect'). It's a lovely idea delivered in one held breath, and it slightly smothers the paragraph's real treasure — the 'I am going to marry him' quote — which arrives after the reader's already winded. Also, fully spelled-out negatives ('did not,' 'almost did not') read stiffer than the warm, spoken tone elsewhere; this couple clearly talks in contractions.
issues.fix: Split the long sentence and let the quote breathe. e.g. 'Meghan barely knew her roommate before moving in — she almost didn't move in at all. So the fact that any of this happened still feels like the ultimate butterfly effect.' Use contractions throughout ('didn't,' 'wasn't') to match the chattier register of paragraph three. Pure text-block edit, no platform limits.

issues.title: The supporting cast is introduced inconsistently across paragraphs
issues.why: Sophia appears in paragraph one ('her friend Sophia') as if we know who she is; then paragraph three reintroduces a roommate as 'Edie (Meghan roommate who introduced them)' — but paragraph one already credited the introduction to roommates dating, without naming Edie. So the person who actually set the whole love story in motion is revealed late, in a parenthetical, while Sophia (a friend) gets earlier billing. For a guest who doesn't know these names, the cast is slightly scrambled, and the loveliest structural irony — the roommate who introduced them was THERE for the proposal — lands softer than it should.
issues.fix: Name Edie's role at first mention in paragraph one ('thanks to Meghan's roommate Edie, whose boyfriend happened to be Owen's roommate' — adjust to the true relationship), so paragraph three can pay it off cleanly: 'Edie — the roommate who introduced us in the first place — was right there when he proposed.' That turns a parenthetical into a full-circle moment. Text-only edit.

issues.title: 'OUR LOVE STORY' and the wordmark do no narrative work
issues.why: The section heading 'OUR LOVE STORY' is the genre default — it labels the content without adding voice, in a site that otherwise has a real voice waiting in the next line. Similarly the wordmark 'MEGHAN & OWEN' is correct and clean but purely nominal; nothing in the top-of-page type tells a guest these are the people who pulled off a 36-hour secret proposal. These are small spots, but they're the first words guests read, set in the elegant sage Cormorant — prime real estate currently spent on labels.
issues.fix: Optionally retitle the story section with something warmer and theirs — 'How We Got Here,' 'The Easiest Yes,' or 'Five Years In' (the last echoes their own line). Keep the wordmark as-is for clarity, but consider a tiny voiced tagline beneath it where the theme allows a text block — e.g. 'married in the Jersey countryside · engaged on a Greek cliff.' Heading text is fully editable in Zola; the tagline depends on whether a text block can sit under the wordmark in this theme — flag as a maybe.

issues.title: No voiced line tells a scrolling guest who/when/where in the couple's own words
issues.why: This overlaps with what the UX and planner reviewers will raise, but it's a STORYTELLING gap too: the hero has no overlaid words, and the names band states 'JUNE 18, 2027 / Whitehouse Station, NJ' as bare facts with no warmth. The first emotional handshake with a guest is delayed until they reach paragraph one. A single voiced line near the top would let the brand greet people instead of just labeling them.
issues.fix: Add one short, warm orienting line in a text block in or just under the names band — e.g. 'Friday, June 18, 2027 — we'd love you in Whitehouse Station, NJ, for the celebration.' It does double duty: it warms the facts AND surfaces the date/place earlier. Trivial in a Zola text block.

conceptSeeds.title: A voice 'thread' that runs the whole site, not one paragraph
conceptSeeds.idea: Pull two recurring micro-voices from paragraph two — her wit, his calm — and let them surface as tiny, consistent touches across every built and future page. Imagine paired captions or a recurring '(Meghan:) … (Owen:) …' aside used sparingly on FAQs and Schedule later — she answers practically and dryly, he answers warmly. Even on the built pages now, one shared voiced line per section turns a template into a couple. The goal isn't more copy; it's the SAME copy carrying their fingerprints.

conceptSeeds.title: Make the proposal a told story with a pull-quote, not a wall of text
conceptSeeds.idea: The love story currently delivers everything in three justified-feeling blocks. Consider lifting their best line — 'It was the easiest yes of all time' or 'I am going to marry him' — into its own large Cormorant pull-quote between the text and the proposal photos (a Zola text block sized up). It gives the eye a rest, rewards skimmers, and makes the page feel art-directed by the people who lived it. Same words, far more impact.

conceptSeeds.title: Let the honeymoon fund become the site's second story beat
conceptSeeds.idea: Right now the Greek proposal is the only narrative. The honeymoon fund is a natural place for a SECOND, forward-looking beat: not 'how we met' but 'where we're going next,' written in their voice. Framed well, it turns a transactional tile into the most-shared sentence on the registry — and quietly seeds the Greek/Aegean motif the other reviewers will want to pull into the visual system. Story and design reinforcing each other, all through copy they can write tonight.

critiques.singleHighestPriority: Write the honeymoon-fund description in the couple's own voice and tie it to the Greek-island proposal. It is a 10-minute, fully Zola-feasible edit that fixes the site's biggest storytelling gap (their voice currently lives in only one paragraph), turns the registry's emotional climax from bare default text into a real moment, and plants the Aegean thread the rest of the site is begging for — the single highest return-on-effort piece of writing on the whole site.

critiques.persona: Theo Nakamura — Photography & Visual Art Director (imagery & Mediterranean aesthetic)
critiques.overallImpression: You two are sitting on a photographic gift most couples would kill for, and I want to start there because it is the whole game. A sunset proposal on a Cycladic clifftop, the blue-domed Church of the Seven Martyrs floating in the sea behind you, white stucco and Aegean light — that is a fully-formed, ownable visual world. The three-panel hero is a genuinely bold editorial move: full-bleed, edge-to-edge, no gutters, ~850px tall, sequenced left-to-right as her reaction / the wide cliff scene / the kiss. When it works, that triptych reads like a film strip of the single best 30 seconds of your relationship, and I love that you trusted the pictures enough to put zero text on top of them. The love-story copy is specific and warm — the 36-hour secret flight, "the easiest yes of all time" — it earns its place. So the raw material is an A. My critique is almost entirely about art direction and discipline, not assets: right now the site is fighting itself. A generic English-cottage watercolor floral system (blush, butter-yellow, sage foliage) is laid over crisp Mediterranean blue-white-stone photography, and the two palettes have nothing to say to each other. The hero also leads with the kiss and reveals who/when/where only after a scroll. And the same proposal set is doing the work of an entire gallery, hero, and story — one location, shown four-plus times across two pages, with no second visual chapter. The fix is not more photos or a redesign; it is sequencing, palette alignment, and letting the Aegean — not the template florals — be the brand. Get the art direction right and this becomes one of the most distinctive wedding sites I have seen.
strengths: The three-panel full-bleed hero is a confident, editorial decision. Edge-to-edge with no gutters at ~850px tall, it reads as a cinematic triptych rather than a generic banner — most Zola sites settle for one safe horizontal photo, and you didn't.

Trusting the images with NO text overlay on the hero is the right instinct photographically. The blue dome, the cliff, the light don't need a headline competing with them — clean photography speaks louder than a caption.

The intra-hero sequencing has real narrative logic: her reaction (left) → the wide proposal scene (center) → the kiss (right). That is essentially a three-frame story arc, and it's a smart way to use a burst of proposal photos.

The proposal photography itself is a rare, ownable asset — a specific, identifiable place (Church of the Seven Martyrs, Kastro, Sifnos) with a strong natural palette. This is the kind of imagery you cannot fake or buy, and it's the spine of any brand we'd build.

Restraint in the lower home page: a small floral sprig as a section divider before the two stacked clifftop photos is a tasteful, quiet transition — proof the couple has an eye for pacing when they slow down.

The love-story copy is genuinely well-written and image-adjacent — it makes a reader want to SEE more of Greece, which means the words and pictures are already pulling in the same direction even if the design system isn't yet.

issues.title: The decorative floral system contradicts your actual photography
issues.why: This is the central visual problem and it's a photographer's issue as much as a designer's. Your images are crisp Mediterranean — Aegean blue, whitewashed stone, butter-warm sunset light, hard Cycladic sun. The site-wide decorative layer is a soft, romantic English-cottage-garden watercolor (blush-pink + cream + butter-yellow blooms, sage foliage) that persists in the bottom-right corner of EVERY page. These two worlds share no palette, no light quality, no place. The watercolor florals are generic to the Zola template — they are not yours — and they actively dilute the one thing that IS yours. Every time a guest sees that blush spray next to your blue-dome photo, the brand gets a little less specific.
issues.fix: Within Zola: lean the theme COLOR away from the floral and toward the photography. Push the sage accent toward a deeper Aegean/Santorini blue drawn straight from the dome in your own hero — a hue pulled from your photos will reconcile the florals far better than the current sage, which belongs to the template, not to you. If the theme lets you reduce or simplify the corner floral, do it; you want it quieter so the photography dominates. If the florals cannot be removed or recolored and they keep clashing, that is the one case where I'd say a THEME SWITCH is justified — to a cleaner, photo-forward Zola theme with a minimal or no permanent decorative spray. Flagging clearly: full removal of the persistent corner floral may not be possible without changing themes (platform limit).

issues.title: The hero leads with the kiss and withholds who/when/where
issues.why: Right now the rightmost of the three panels is the kiss, shown immediately, and there is no name or date anywhere on the hero — guests must scroll past ~850px of photo to reach the names band and learn it's Meghan & Owen, June 18 2027, Whitehouse Station NJ. Two problems. First, narratively you've spent your emotional climax (the kiss) in the first three seconds; there's nowhere left to build. Second, a wedding hero has a job beyond beauty — orientation. A guest landing cold should know whose wedding and when within one screen. The crisp Cycladic image is strong enough to carry a quiet title; you're leaving that work undone.
issues.fix: Two Zola-feasible moves. (1) Reconsider the panel order so the hero builds rather than peaks instantly — e.g. the wide cliff/ocean establishing shot as the anchor (center or left), her reaction, and let the kiss resolve last or move it down into the body where it lands as a payoff. (2) Even with no text baked onto the photo, you can let the names band do hero duty: ensure 'MEGHAN & OWEN — JUNE 18, 2027 — Whitehouse Station, NJ' sits immediately below the fold edge so it's nearly in view, or choose a header image whose composition has negative sky where the wordmark could visually anchor. At minimum, the date+location should not require a meaningful scroll to discover.

issues.title: One location is carrying the entire visual brand — no second chapter
issues.area: Image program across Home & Gallery strategy
issues.why: The same Sifnos proposal set appears in the 3-panel hero AND again as two stacked photos lower on Home — the identical clifftop/church, four-plus times across the built pages. As beautiful as it is, repetition flattens a strong image into wallpaper. The eye stops registering it. Photographically you have a one-note program: one place, one moment, one light. A wedding visual identity needs range — different scales (wide/detail), different subjects (the two of you outside the proposal, hands/ring, the island, your everyday selves), so the brand has texture and the gallery later has somewhere to go.
issues.fix: On Home now: cut the redundancy. Keep the triptych hero OR the two stacked photos as the proposal beat — don't do both with the same frames. Replace the second placement with a DIFFERENT register: a detail (the ring, hands, a wide quiet landscape of the island with no people) so the page has variety rather than echo. Going forward, deliberately shoot/collect a broader set — engagement-style portraits in your real life, NJ/home imagery to tie the actual wedding location to the Greek story — so the Mediterranean is the emotional anchor, not the only note. Curation is fully in your control on Zola even though the gallery grid UI isn't.

issues.title: The honeymoon-fund tile has no photo and no destination — a missed Mediterranean callback
issues.why: At the very bottom of a 5,350px registry sits a single small 'Honeymoon Fund!' cash tile with NO description and NO imagery of the honeymoon or destination. From a visual-storytelling view this is a wasted beat: you have the most evocative travel photography imaginable already in hand (or the exact aesthetic established), and the one place a honeymoon image belongs is blank. It also reads as an afterthought tucked under 33 kitchen items, so the romantic ask gets buried beneath cookware.
issues.fix: Within Zola you control the honeymoon fund's image and copy: give it a single strong Mediterranean-toned photo (a wide Aegean horizon, a whitewashed-village frame — even from the same trip) and 2-3 sentences naming where you dream of going and what guests would be funding (a sunset dinner, a sail between islands). This ties the registry back to the proposal story and makes the romantic ask feel intentional, not bolted on.

issues.title: Pure-white everywhere wastes the chance to let the Aegean palette breathe
issues.why: Every page is pure white (#FFFFFF) background. White is a defensible gallery choice and it does let photos pop — but combined with the full-bleed hero, it means your one and only color story lives entirely inside the photographs and then evaporates the instant the images end. There's no environmental color carrying the Cycladic mood through the rest of the page. The result: islands of beautiful blue-white photography floating in a sea of generic white with mismatched blush florals in the corner.
issues.fix: Zola theme color won't repaint full backgrounds, but you CAN extend the palette through the levers you do control: set the theme accent to a sea-blue or warm-stone tone pulled from your photos (this recolors headings/buttons/links), and consider one full-bleed photo band lower on the page (Zola photo blocks can go edge-to-edge in many themes) so the Aegean color reappears as a deliberate rhythm — photo / white / photo — rather than one hero and then nothing. Let the photography, not the florals, be the recurring color event.
