issues.title: Sage Cormorant on white — verify the headings actually hold up against the photography
issues.why: Cormorant Garamond 400, all-caps, ~2px tracking, in sage #505956 on white is elegant and I like the editorial restraint. But two visual-direction cautions: (1) sage #505956 on white sits near the WCAG AA line for the lighter, thin all-caps treatment — fine for large display, riskier for smaller instances and on mobile. (2) More to my lens: a muted sage wordmark sitting on white directly above a vivid blue-and-white photograph can look tonally disconnected — the brand color and the image color aren't related. If you shift the accent toward a hue actually present in your photos, the type stops looking like it belongs to a different project than the pictures.
issues.fix: Test the heading color the moment you change the accent: a deeper sea-blue or charcoal-stone pulled from your hero will both improve contrast over sage AND visually tie the wordmark to the photography. Keep Cormorant — it's working — just let its color come from your own images. Confirm legibility on a phone in daylight, since that's where most guests will see it.

issues.title: No RSVP path — separate from my lens, but I'm flagging it because it affects the hero's job
issues.why: There's no RSVP anywhere in the nav. I'm not the IA reviewer, but it touches my lens: a wedding hero and home page exist partly to funnel a guest to two actions — know the date, and respond. The photography is doing the seduction beautifully; there's currently nothing for that emotional momentum to convert into.
issues.fix: Enable Zola's RSVP page and add it to the nav (Zola supports this directly — no platform stretch). Once it exists, you could even let a strong photo headline the RSVP page so the visual brand carries all the way through to the action, not just the landing.

issues.title: Decorative motifs repeat per-page while the photography doesn't vary — backwards priority
issues.why: The watercolor system is consistent and recurring (corner spray on every page, sprig divider, footer motif) while the photography is the thing that repeats without variation. So the GENERIC element is your consistent brand thread and the OWNABLE element is the monotonous one. That's exactly inverted from what you want: the florals should recede and vary in weight, while the photography should be the consistent, evolving signature across pages.
issues.fix: As you build the other pages, make a single recurring photographic device your through-line — e.g. each page header gets one full-bleed Mediterranean-toned image — so the BRAND thread becomes your photography, and dial the florals back to a single quiet placement. This is achievable with Zola photo/header blocks and page-by-page hero image choices; no custom code needed.

conceptSeeds.title: Build the gallery as 'Greece → Home' — two chapters, two palettes
conceptSeeds.idea: When the Gallery comes online, art-direct it as a deliberate two-act sequence rather than a dump of files. Act I: the Cycladic proposal world — wide establishing island shots, the church and dome, the clifftop, the kiss, details (ring, hands, the Aegean). Act II: bring it home — your everyday life as a couple and imagery that ties to Whitehouse Station NJ and the June wedding. The narrative is the whole hook of your story (he flew 36 hours to a Greek island; now you're getting married in New Jersey), and the gallery is the one page that can show that arc visually. Sequence wide → tight, establish → intimate, within each act. Use Zola gallery blocks; the curation and order are entirely yours.

conceptSeeds.title: Let one photographic device be the brand spine across all pages
conceptSeeds.idea: Pick a single repeating photographic treatment — e.g. every page opens with one full-bleed, edge-to-edge image in a consistent warm-Aegean tone — and make THAT your recognizable signature instead of the corner florals. Travel page opens on an island road; Schedule on a sunlit table; FAQs on a quiet horizon. Consistent crop discipline (all wide cinematic, all the same height) turns a collection of pages into one cohesive visual brand carried entirely by photography. Fully Zola-feasible via per-page header/photo blocks.

conceptSeeds.title: Pull every accent color out of your own hero
conceptSeeds.idea: Do a literal eyedropper exercise on your best proposal frame: the dome blue, the stone white, the sunset butter, the deep sea. That four-color set IS your wedding palette and it's already perfect because it's real and it's yours. Map Zola's single theme accent to the strongest of those (the sea-blue), and let copy, the honeymoon-fund tile, and any future detail pages echo it. The goal: a guest should be able to look at the photos and the color of the words and feel they came from the same afternoon on Sifnos — which is the opposite of the current template-sage-plus-blush-floral situation.

critiques.singleHighestPriority: Reconcile the decorative palette with your photography — let the Aegean be the brand, not the template florals. Concretely: shift Zola's theme accent color from the generic template sage to a sea-blue/warm-stone tone eyedropped from your own hero, quiet down (or, if the theme forces it, switch themes to drop) the persistent blush English-cottage-garden corner floral, and stop letting a generic decorative layer outshine the single most ownable asset you have. Everything else — hero sequencing, gallery chapters, the honeymoon tile — gets easier once the site's color story comes from your photographs instead of fighting them.

concepts.tagline: Engaged on a Greek cliff. Married in the Jersey countryside. The sea is the through-line.
concepts.bigIdea: Make the place and the proposal the brand. Right now Meghan and Owen own one of the most distinctive wedding stories a couple can have — Owen flew 36 hours in secret to a Cycladic clifftop, an entire island conspired in the surprise, and she said "the easiest yes of all time" at sunset with the blue-domed Church of the Seven Martyrs floating in the Aegean behind them. But that story is quarantined: it lives only in the hero photos and one paragraph, while the actual visual identity is a generic blush-and-butter English-cottage watercolor that belongs to a different wedding entirely. The single most-cited problem across all six critics is the same — the warm cottage-garden florals (and the sage accent that agrees with them) are at war with the cool, crisp, blue-white-stone photography. The Aegean Thread resolves that war decisively: we retire the borrowed garden and let the Cyclades become the design system. The color of the words becomes the color of the place Owen proposed. The thread is literal and structural — it runs from the home hero (the sea), through the love story (where it happened), to a reframed honeymoon fund ("Send Us Back to the Aegean") that closes the loop the proposal opens, and forward through every page as a single recurring photographic and chromatic signature. The narrative spine: a butterfly-effect meet-cute → engaged on a Greek island → coming home to marry in New Jersey → returning to the sea together. Crucially, this is not a Greece-themed wedding (the wedding is in Whitehouse Station, NJ); it is a Greece-rooted love story told with restraint, so the Aegean is the emotional anchor and New Jersey is the destination — both honored, neither faked.
concepts.visualLanguage: Restrained Cycladic, not kitsch Santorini. The reference is the editorial calm the type system already reaches for (think Cereal/Kinfolk travel pages on the Greek islands) — whitewashed wall, one true Aegean blue, warm stone, a thread of olive, sun-bleached neutrals — drawn straight from the couple's own frames rather than stock. The discipline is the point: the photography carries the color story and the white space lets it breathe, so the site reads "unmistakably Meghan and Owen on Sifnos" instead of "default floral template." Photography becomes the consistent brand thread (the thing that repeats and evolves across pages), while decoration recedes to near-nothing — the inverse of today, where the generic florals are the consistent element and the ownable photos are the monotonous one. Practical levers inside Zola: (1) set the theme accent to a sea-blue eyedropped from the actual church dome in their hero, so headings, links, and buttons inherit the color of the place; (2) let the engagement set, edited for a cool blue-white-stone palette, be the recurring visual event — a full-bleed image band on the home page and one consistent header image atop every built-out page; (3) introduce rhythm by alternating photo / white / photo rather than floating islands of imagery in an unbroken white field; (4) replace borrowed ornament with a typographic mark — an ampersand or "M&O" set in Cormorant — used sparingly as a divider and footer motif, a mark that is unambiguously theirs and costs nothing. Honest flag, raised by every critic who touched it: the persistent corner floral is baked into this template's art and usually cannot be recolored or removed individually in Zola. The accent-color shift plus photo-forward curation will substantially narrow the clash and is the no-regrets first move; if, after eyedropping a Cycladic blue and leaning hard on cool imagery, the blush spray still actively fights the photos on every page, a switch to a cleaner/more minimal or Mediterranean Zola theme is the one place a theme change is genuinely justified — and should be the deliberate decision, made with eyes open, not an accident.
palette: Aegean Blue #2E6F95 — primary accent, eyedropped in spirit from the church dome; set as the Zola theme color (replaces sage). Headings, links, buttons, active nav inherit it.
Deep Sea #1B4965 — darker blue for emphasis / hover / a heavier heading weight where the theme allows; ties the type to the deepest water in the photos.
Whitewashed #FFFFFF — keep the pure-white background; it now reads as Cycladic stucco rather than generic, because the accent and imagery finally explain it.
Warm Stone #E8E0D4 — sun-bleached neutral for any section-band or photo-mat tone the theme offers; softens the white-to-photo transition.
Olive #6E7651 — a single restrained warm-cool bridge note (foliage in the photos), useful if a secondary accent or divider tint is available; keeps the palette from going cold.

Sunset Ochre #C98A3C — sparing warm highlight pulled from the proposal light; use only as a tiny touch (e.g., keep the gold 'most wanted' star, which already harmonizes) so the palette has warmth and isn't clinically blue.
Near-Black #0E0E0E — unchanged body/UI text color; the critics confirm it carries contrast, so all running copy and the countdown stay here, never light gray.
concepts.typography: Keep the type system almost exactly as-is — it is the one part of the current site every critic praised, and it will sit even better against blue-white than against florals. Display/headings: Cormorant Garamond (already in use; a standard Zola serif option), weight 400, all-caps with ~2px tracking, for the wordmark, page H1s, the date, and the story heading — the fashion-masthead poise stays. Body/UI: Circular (Zola default sans), ~16px, near-black, for nav, paragraphs, buttons, and the countdown. The only changes are color and discipline, not fonts. (1) Recolor the display type from sage #505956 to Aegean Blue #2E6F95 so the wordmark visually belongs to the photographs instead of a different project; verify the chosen blue clears WCAG AA on white (a mid-deep blue like #2E6F95 / #1B4965 comfortably beats sage's ~5–6:1 and reads better on a phone in daylight). (2) Reserve all-caps thin Cormorant for genuinely large display only — wordmark, H1, the names; for any small label or sub-heading, either bump the size or fall back to near-black Circular rather than shrinking the airy serif. (3) Never set paragraph-length copy in all-caps (the love-story body is already sans — keep it). (4) Give the date and countdown real typographic weight: render "Friday, June 18, 2027" large in Cormorant blue as the band's anchor, and either simplify the live timer to days-only or, if it can't be removed, demote it firmly beneath the date in full near-black so the single most-scanned fact stops competing with ticking seconds. If a future "M&O" or ampersand motif is wanted, build it from Cormorant inside text blocks — no new typeface needed.
concepts.homepage: Reorder so the page hosts before it swoons. Top to bottom: (1) HEADER — wordmark "MEGHAN & OWEN" in Cormorant now in Aegean Blue, with the nav including RSVP (see nav). Consider a tiny voiced tagline beneath the wordmark where the theme allows a text block: "married in the Jersey countryside · engaged on a Greek cliff" — instant orientation and instant personality. (2) HERO — keep the bold full-bleed photographic band, but fix the sequencing the photographers flagged: let it BUILD instead of spending the climax first. Reorder the panels left-to-right to setup → scene → reaction (the wide clifftop/church establishing shot leading, the kiss resolving last or moved into the body), so the emotional payoff arrives after the setup, not before it. Reduce the ~850px height somewhat so the facts clear the fold sooner, especially on mobile. (3) ORIENTATION LINE — immediately under the hero, a slim text block in the couple's voice that doubles as the who/when/where: "Friday, June 18, 2027 — we'd love you in Whitehouse Station, NJ. (Yes, a Friday — come make a weekend of it.)" This single line solves three high-severity critiques at once: surfaces the date/location above the long scroll, flags the Friday, and warms the bare facts. (4) NAMES BAND — names stacked in Cormorant blue, "FRIDAY, JUNE 18, 2027" rendered large as the anchor, location, then a quieted days-only countdown (or none). (5) OUR LOVE STORY — keep the genuinely excellent copy, lightly edited (see voice): fix the missing apostrophes ("Meghan's roommate," "Sophia's mom"), split paragraph one's long breath-holding sentence, name Edie's role at first mention so paragraph three can pay it off, and lift one line — "It was the easiest yes of all time" — into a large Cormorant pull-quote between the text and the photos to reward skimmers and give the eye a rest. (6) A SECOND VISUAL REGISTER — the photographers' note: don't show the same Sifnos frame four-plus times. Replace the redundant stacked clifftop pair with a different register (a detail — the ring, hands — or a quiet wide island/sea landscape) so the page has texture, not echo. (7) EXIT RAMP — a closing block at the warmest moment (right after the story) with inline buttons to the next jobs: "RSVP" (or "RSVPs open soon"), "Plan your trip," "See the registry" — capturing intent instead of dumping the guest into the footer. (8) FOOTER — standard Zola, optionally with the "M&O" ampersand motif replacing the floral sprig if achievable in a text block.
concepts.navAndIA: Resolve the missing RSVP — the most-repeated high-severity issue on the whole site. Enable Zola's native RSVP page and add "RSVP" to the nav as the visually terminal action (rightmost), so the row reads Home | Schedule | Travel | Registry | Wedding Party | Gallery | FAQs | RSVP. Until invitations go out (the couple is ~13 months out, so it's reasonable it isn't open yet), the page holds a dated promise in their voice: "RSVPs open in early 2027 — for now, save Friday evening, June 18." The slot existing now sets guest expectation and the IA's contract about what this site can do. Pressure-test the row: eight items is past the comfortable single-row limit, and Zola typically collapses to a hamburger on mobile — confirm the mobile order leads with RSVP and Home. Where who/when/where lives: today it's buried below a wordless ~850px hero; the redesign puts it in three places so it's unmissable — the optional wordmark tagline, the orientation line directly under the hero, and the names band — guaranteeing the date and city are visible without a deliberate scroll on a phone. Render the date everywhere as "Friday, June 18, 2027" (the day-of-week is load-bearing for travel/time-off and should never be discoverable only by checking a calendar). Add the venue name to the names band once known, not just the town. Keep the underlined active-state but verify it's distinguishable enough as the sole wayfinding cue; use a bolder active treatment if the theme offers one. All of this is native Zola — no theme switch, no custom code.
signatureMoments: The color of the place becomes the color of the site. Eyedropping the church-dome blue into the Zola theme accent so the wordmark, headings, and buttons are literally the blue of the cliff where Owen proposed — the instant a guest who's seen the photos lands on the page, identity and story are the same thing. This is the single highest-leverage move and the lever that turns 'elegant default' into 'unmistakably theirs.'

A Cormorant pull-quote: 'It was the easiest yes of all time.' Lifting their own best line into large blue display type between the story and the photos — an art-directed beat that rewards skimmers, gives the page rhythm, and makes the home page feel authored by the people who lived it rather than templated.

'Send Us Back to the Aegean' — the honeymoon fund as the story's third act. Reframing the bare, undescribed cash tile (currently buried at position 34 under 33 kitchen items) into a titled, photo-backed, voiced moment near the TOP of the registry: a blue-dome image, a line of Cormorant, and copy that names the dream — 'We got engaged on a clifftop in the Cyclades at sunset, and we're not done with the Greek islands yet. Help send us back to the Aegean as a married couple (yes, we'll find that little blue-domed church again).' Closes the narrative loop the proposal opens and converts the single most on-brand gift from generic to the most-clicked thing on the page.

An 'At a glance' guest card high on the home page. A compact one-screen block answering the 5-second job — Friday, June 18, 2027 · Whitehouse Station, NJ · 'RSVP by [date]' · a save-the-date/calendar affordance — turning a scroll-to-find experience into a screenshot-and-go one for out-of-town guests on their phones.

One photographic device as the brand spine. Every built-out page opens with a single full-bleed, cool-toned Aegean-or-home image at a consistent crop and height — so the recognizable through-line across the site becomes their photography, while the borrowed florals recede to a single quiet placement. The generic stops being the consistent element; the ownable starts.

pageConcepts.concept: Reorder to host-then-swoon: blue-recolored wordmark + optional tagline, a hero that builds (wide establishing shot leads, kiss resolves), an orientation line surfacing Friday/June 18/Whitehouse Station in the couple's voice, a names band anchored by the large date, the lightly-edited love story with an 'easiest yes' pull-quote, a fresh second photographic register (ring/hands or a quiet sea landscape — not a fourth repeat of the same clifftop), and an exit-ramp block of next-step buttons. Solves the buried who/when/where, the kiss-first hero, the repeated single location, and the cold dead-end into the footer.

Same chrome (Zola-templated, limited control), better curation and copy. Pin a titled, photo-backed 'Send Us Back to the Aegean' honeymoon fund near the top right after the 'most wanted' Le Creuset, with a real 2–3 sentence story (optionally split into concrete experience tiles — 'a sunset dinner on the water,' 'a day on a boat in the Cyclades' — which convert better than one open pool). Lead the grid with the most beautiful objects (Le Creuset, Pillivuyt, glassware); relocate purely utilitarian items (the O-Cedar spin mop, basic storage) lower or trim them; keep budget-friendly entry points but swap the most chore-like for charming-but-affordable ones (linen napkins, a good pepper mill, a cookbook they love, a candle). Add a short voiced registry intro line. Add a couple more 'most wanted' stars at varied price points to anchor decisions across budgets, and broaden 2–4 items beyond the kitchen monoculture so non-cookware givers have a fitting gift.

When built, the place to own the Friday logistics warmly: a clear weekend-at-a-glance timeline with a one-line 'it's a Friday evening — we hope you'll make a weekend of it' framing, each section topped by the consistent full-bleed cool-toned header image. A natural home for the couple's two-voice device (Meghan's practical, dry answers; Owen's warm ones).

The hospitality workhorse for a rural Hunterdon County location most guests won't know: nearest airport and rough drive time, hotel blocks, whether to plan a Thursday/Friday arrival — opened with an island-road or NJ-countryside header image to keep the Aegean-to-home thread visible. Pre-answers the four questions every out-of-towner is about to text.

A chance to bring the 'whole island was in on it' warmth forward: short, voiced bios that nod to the supporting cast already in the story — Sophia and her co-conspirator mom, Edie the roommate-who-introduced-them — under a consistent header image. Turns a roster into part of the narrative.

Art-direct as a deliberate two-act arc rather than a photo dump — 'Greece → Home.' Act I: the Cycladic proposal world (wide establishing island shots, the church and dome, the clifftop, the kiss, details — ring, hands, the Aegean), sequenced wide→tight. Act II: bring it home — everyday-couple imagery and frames tying to Whitehouse Station and the June wedding. Optionally a small 'How he pulled it off' sequence on Owen's 36-hour journey. This is the one page that can show the whole story visually and give the repeated proposal set somewhere to expand.

The findability safety net and the funniest use of the two-voice device: absorb every question the nav can't answer in one tap — dress code, kids/plus-ones, parking, the Friday timing, the 'where exactly is Whitehouse Station' question. Group by theme, lead with the highest-frequency, written in their proven warm-and-witty register so even logistics feel like hospitality.

concepts.copyVoice: Treat voice as a system, not a single paragraph — its biggest current failure is that Meghan and Owen sound like themselves for ~150 words of love story and like default Zola everywhere else. The register is already proven in paragraph three: specific, warm, funny, in contractions, with a dry type-A wit (Meghan) balanced by calm romance (Owen) — both literally named in the copy, so let both be performed, not just described. Concrete moves: (1) Let the voice leak, sparingly, into microcopy guests actually read first — a one-line welcome ('We're getting married — and somehow we talked an entire Greek island into helping ask'), the orientation line under the hero, a voiced registry intro, the honeymoon-fund description, and a closing footer line if a text block allows. (2) Optionally retitle 'OUR LOVE STORY' to something theirs — 'The Easiest Yes,' 'How We Got Here,' or 'Five Years In' (echoing their own line). (3) A two-voice device — an occasional '(Meghan:) … (Owen:) …' aside on FAQs and Schedule, she answering practically and dryly, he warmly — turns template pages into a couple. (4) Proofread the love story: fix 'Meghan roommate' → 'Meghan's roommate,' 'Sophia mom' → 'Sophia's mom,' confirm Edie/Sophia spelling. (5) Tighten prose rhythm: split paragraph one's ~45-word sentence so the 'I am going to marry him' quote lands with air around it, keep contractions throughout, and name Edie's introducing role early so paragraph three's 'the roommate who introduced us was right there when he proposed' becomes a full-circle payoff. Protect the gold: 'I am going to marry him. Five years later, here we are' and 'the easiest yes of all time' are ownable, quotable, this-couple lines — do not sand them down.
concepts.feasibilityOnZola: Most of this is no-code, this-weekend work inside Zola's documented controls — and that's deliberate, because Zola forbids arbitrary HTML/CSS. NATIVE, DO NOW (no theme switch): change the theme accent color to the Aegean blue (Zola supports a custom theme hex), which recolors the wordmark, headings, links, buttons, and active nav in one move; enable the RSVP page and add it to the nav (rightmost); reorder/relocate the hero photo panels and registry items, including pinning and describing the honeymoon fund; add text and button blocks for the orientation line, pull-quote, 'at a glance' card, exit ramp, and voiced microcopy; render the date as 'Friday, June 18, 2027'; edit all love-story copy; broaden and re-curate registry items; choose cool-toned hero/header/fund images; build an 'M&O' ampersand mark inside a Cormorant text block as a divider/footer motif; set one consistent full-bleed header image per page as content is built. THEME-DEPENDENT (verify in their theme, no code): whether a text block can sit directly under the wordmark for the tagline; whether the live countdown's granularity can be reduced to days-only or hidden; whether a bolder active-nav treatment or a non-white section band/photo-mat tone is available; whether photo blocks can go truly edge-to-edge for the alternating photo/white rhythm. HONEST LIMITS / FLAGS: the persistent blush corner floral, the sprig divider, and the footer motif are baked into this template's art and generally cannot be individually recolored or removed in Zola — the accent shift plus photo-forward curation narrows the clash substantially and is the no-regrets path, but full removal is the one scenario where a deliberate theme switch (to a cleaner/minimal or Mediterranean Zola theme) is justified, flagged clearly because a switch will reset layout and every page must be re-checked afterward. The registry grid's chrome (filter row, card layout, Zola toolbar) is not stylable — only curation, the most-wanted star, the fund, and copy are in the couple's control. No custom fonts beyond Zola's set (Cormorant + Circular are already available, so the type plan needs nothing new).
concepts.risks: Tradeoffs, named honestly. (1) The floral clash may not fully resolve without a theme switch. The accent-color change reconciles the palette a lot, but if the blush spray still fights cool photography on every page, the couple faces a real fork: live with a narrowed-but-present tension, or switch themes and re-do layout/re-check all pages — a meaningful afternoon-plus of work that can reset spacing and hero behavior. Recommend doing the accent + curation first and judging the residual clash before deciding. (2) 'Cycladic' can tip into kitsch. The whole direction depends on restraint — one true blue, real photos, lots of white. Over-applied (blue everything, Santorini clichés, a Greece-themed wedding the wedding isn't), it cheapens fast and could confuse guests about where the wedding actually is (New Jersey). Keep NJ honored in copy and let Greece be the emotional root, not a costume. (3) Accent-color contrast must be checked. A blue that's too light fails WCAG AA on white and on phones in sunlight; pick a mid-deep blue (~#2E6F95 or darker) and verify, especially for the thin all-caps Cormorant. (4) Mobile reflow of the photo hero. A multi-panel band can stack awkwardly and push facts down; the orientation line and reduced hero height mitigate this, but it must be tested on a real phone. (5) Photo dependency. The two-act gallery and the 'one image per page' spine assume a broader, cool-edited photo set exists; if only the proposal frames are available, the brand stays one-note — worth deliberately collecting more (everyday-couple, NJ, details) over the 13-month runway. (6) Voice overreach. The two-voice device and jokes are charming in small doses; over-used they get cute and tiring, and the dry type-A wit shouldn't read as sharp to family — keep it sparing and warm. (7) Don't over-edit the love story. The copy fixes are real, but the personality is the best asset on the site; the risk is sanding it into competent blandness. Edit for apostrophes, rhythm, and the Edie payoff — protect the voice.

concepts.directionName: Quiet Editorial — "The Aegean, Set in Whitespace"
concepts.tagline: Engaged on a Greek cliff, married in the Jersey countryside — a site as clean and sunlit as the morning he flew 36 hours to surprise her.
concepts.bigIdea: Treat the website like a single, beautifully art-directed magazine feature about one couple — not a flowery template with their photos dropped in. The premise: maximum restraint so the engagement photography carries the entire emotional load. The site should feel like turning the pages of Kinfolk or Cereal — full-bleed Cycladic imagery, generous white margins, refined type, and almost no ornament. The panel's loudest, most-repeated finding becomes our north star: the warm English-cottage-garden florals are fighting the crisp Aegean blue-white-stone photography, and they are generic to the template. Quiet Editorial resolves that war by removing the fight entirely — we strip ornament down to near-zero and let the photographs (which ARE personal to this couple) become the only decoration and the recurring brand thread. Where five reviewers wanted to ADD a Mediterranean motif, this direction's discipline says: don't add a competing layer at all; subtract the florals, pull the single accent color straight out of the church dome, and let the pictures and the white space do everything. The story spine — butterfly-effect meet-cute → "I'm going to marry him" → 36-hour secret proposal on Sifnos → easiest yes of all time → returning to the Aegean for the honeymoon — runs through the site as the editorial through-line, told in their own voice and resolved at the honeymoon fund. Her type-A wit and his calm romance become the magazine's editorial voice; the design simply gets out of the way of the images and the facts.
concepts.visualLanguage: Gallery-grade minimalism. The hardest-working rule: photography is the brand, ornament is the enemy. (1) Subtract the florals. Quiet Editorial cannot coexist with a persistent blush watercolor spray in every bottom-right corner — that decorative layer is the single biggest obstacle to this direction and, per the dossier, it is baked into the template and usually can't be removed or recolored individually in Zola. So this direction is honest up front: it likely requires a THEME SWITCH to a clean, photo-forward, low/no-ornament Zola theme (think editorial/minimal, white background, no fixed botanical art). That is the one platform stretch this direction depends on, and it's worth it. (2) Photography sets the rhythm. Full-bleed, edge-to-edge images alternate with deep fields of white in a deliberate cadence: image / white / image. No drop shadows, no rounded-corner photo cards, no borders. Every photo bleeds to the edge or sits in a clean rectangle with wide margins. (3) One accent, eyedropped from the photos. The single Zola theme color is pulled directly from the blue dome of the Church of the Seven Martyrs — so the color of the words is literally the color of the place he proposed (a point five reviewers independently reached). (4) A typographic signature replaces the floral sprig. Section dividers and the recurring "mark" become a Cormorant ampersand or "M & O" set large in the accent blue — an authored device instead of borrowed botanical furniture. (5) Type as architecture. Generous letter-spacing, narrow measures, lots of air. The layout reads as composed, not decorated. The overall feeling: sunlit, calm, confident, expensive — a site that looks like it was art-directed by the two people who lived the story.
palette: #FFFFFF — Gallery white (background, every page; the negative space is a design element, not emptiness)

#1E5F8C — Aegean dome blue (PRIMARY ACCENT — eyedropped from the Church of the Seven Martyrs dome; set as the Zola theme color; used for headings, the wordmark, links, buttons, the typographic ampersand)
#0E0E0E — Near-black ink (all body copy, nav, UI, and — critically — the date/location/countdown so guest-critical facts never inherit a thin or low-contrast treatment)
#103A52 — Deep harbor navy (optional darker tint of the accent for small headings/labels where the mid-blue would dip under WCAG AA; a safe legibility fallback)

#EDE7DD — Warm whitewashed stone (a barely-there off-white from the Cycladic stucco, for the occasional full-width section band so the page has a soft second 'paper' without introducing color clutter)

#C9742E — Sunset terracotta (a sparing, optional micro-accent pulled from the proposal sunset — used at most once or twice, e.g. a single 'most wanted' or honeymoon-fund highlight; never as a system color)
concepts.typography: Keep the type system almost exactly as built — every reviewer agreed it's the strongest non-photo asset, and it sits even better against blue-and-white than against florals. DISPLAY: Cormorant Garamond, weight 400, ALL-CAPS, ~2px tracking, but recolored from sage #505956 to Aegean blue #1E5F8C so the wordmark visually belongs to the photography instead of the discarded florals. Reserve this treatment for genuinely large display only: the wordmark, page H1s, the names, and pull-quotes. BODY/UI: keep Circular (Zola default sans), ~16px, near-black #0E0E0E — clean, readable, the correct neutral counterpoint to the serif display. Three disciplined rules drawn from the panel: (1) Never set anything below large display size in thin all-caps Cormorant blue — for small headings/labels, either bump the size or fall back to the harbor-navy tint (#103A52) or near-black sans, because mid-blue Cormorant at small sizes risks dipping under AA on white and the thin all-caps strokes get airy on a phone in sunlight. (2) All-caps is for short titles only, never sentences — body copy stays sentence-case sans. (3) The date and countdown get weight and size, not delicacy — they are the most-scanned facts on the page and must read as focal, in solid near-black, not 'small light sans.' Realistic on Zola: Cormorant + Circular and a custom theme hex are both within Zola's font/color controls; the recolor is a settings change, not a stretch.
concepts.homepage: A magazine cover that orients first, then seduces. TOP — Masthead + facts in one breath: the wordmark MEGHAN & OWEN (Cormorant, Aegean blue) sits above a single-row nav, and immediately a slim, quiet caption line gives the three orientation facts the panel said are buried: "Friday, June 18, 2027 · Whitehouse Station, NJ." Note the deliberate copy fix — "Friday" is spelled out (Eleanor's load-bearing point: a Friday wedding reshapes every guest's travel and time-off decisions). HERO — One image, not three. Quiet Editorial favors a single full-bleed establishing shot: the wide clifftop scene with the blue-domed church floating in the Aegean behind them. This fixes three problems at once — it reflows predictably on mobile (Marcus/Devin), it stops spending the emotional climax (the kiss) in the first three seconds and withholding who/when/where (Theo/Eleanor), and it's the most gallery-like, art-directed choice. The kiss and her-reaction frames move down into the body as the payoff. If they love the triptych, the fallback is to keep it on desktop but re-sequence left→right as setup → scene → reaction/kiss so the page builds rather than peaks, and confirm the mobile stack leads with the strongest single frame. THE FACTS BAND — directly under the hero, tightened up the page: the names stacked in large Cormorant blue, then "FRIDAY, JUNE 18, 2027," then "Whitehouse Station, NJ," then a calm countdown shown in DAYS only (not ticking seconds — Eleanor/Devin), in solid near-black at a confident size, with a primary RSVP button beneath it (the keystone fix — see nav). A one-line warm welcome in their voice sits here too: "We're getting married — and somehow we talked an entire Greek island into helping ask." A SHORT 'KNOW BEFORE YOU GO' STRIP — three to four sentences (not a critique of the empty Travel page, just a teaser on a built page): "We're marrying in the New Jersey countryside, about an hour from Newark. It's a Friday evening, so make a weekend of it — hotel blocks and a timeline are coming soon." OUR LOVE STORY — kept largely as written (it's the best asset on the site), with light copy polish: fix the missing apostrophes ("Meghan's roommate," "Sophia's mom"), split paragraph one's 45-word breath-holder, use contractions, and name Edie's introducing role up front so paragraph three's "the roommate who introduced us was right there when he proposed" lands as a full-circle moment. A large Cormorant pull-quote in Aegean blue — "It was the easiest yes of all time." — sits between the prose and the photos as the eye's rest stop and the typographic divider (replacing the floral sprig). THE PROPOSAL FRAMES — now the payoff: the kiss and reaction frames, plus one frame in a different register (a detail — the ring, hands — or a quiet wide island landscape) so the page has variety instead of echo (Theo). EXIT RAMP — a closing block at peak emotion with three text links: "RSVP," "Plan your trip," "See the registry" (Devin — capture intent before dumping into the footer). FOOTER — Zola's, with a single voiced line above it if the new theme allows a closing text block.
concepts.navAndIA: Resolve the missing RSVP — this is the unanimous #1 structural fix and it's fully native to Zola, no theme switch or code needed. Enable Zola's RSVP page and add it to the nav. Placement: make it the visually terminal, emphasized item so it reads as the primary action — recommended order "Home · Our Story · Schedule · Travel · Registry · Wedding Party · Gallery · FAQs · RSVP" with RSVP styled distinctly (and, on the home page, mirrored as the one accent-blue button). Honest IA tradeoff the panel flagged: adding RSVP makes the row 8–9 items, past the comfortable single-row limit, so on mobile confirm Zola's hamburger collapse leads with Home and RSVP, and consider whether Gallery can sit lower in priority once it has content. Until invitations go out (~13 months of runway), the RSVP page holds a dated promise in their voice: "RSVPs open in early 2027 — for now, save Friday evening, June 18." WHO/WHEN/WHERE now lives in three places instead of one-buried-spot: (1) the slim caption line directly under the masthead, visible without scrolling; (2) the tightened facts band under the single hero; and (3) the home-page 'Know Before You Go' strip. Keep the underlined active-state — it's the right pattern — but verify the underline is distinguishable enough on the new theme, and use a bolder active treatment if the theme offers one. The wordmark remains the stable 'home' affordance. Net effect: the site stops being an informational keepsake and becomes a working host — a guest can answer "when, where, and how do I respond" in one screen.
signatureMoments: The single-image cover. One full-bleed establishing shot — the clifftop, the blue dome, the Aegean — with the names, 'FRIDAY, JUNE 18, 2027,' and Whitehouse Station, NJ reading immediately beneath it. The most gallery-like, mobile-safe, orientation-first hero possible, and it lets the kiss become a payoff later instead of a spoiler up top.

The Aegean accent. The Zola theme color is eyedropped straight from the church dome, so every heading, link, and button is literally the color of the place Owen proposed. The instant a guest who has seen the photos lands on the page, identity and story become the same thing — achieved with a single hex change.

The pull-quote as divider. 'It was the easiest yes of all time.' set large in Cormorant blue, standing alone between the love story and the proposal frames — a typographic mark that is unmistakably theirs, replacing the generic floral sprig and rewarding skimmers.

The honeymoon fund as Act III. Reframed and moved up near the 'most wanted' anchor: a blue-domed photo, a title with personality ('Send Us Back to the Aegean'), and a real description that closes the story loop — met by butterfly effect, engaged on Sifnos, returning together as a married couple. The site's highest-emotion conversion, currently a blank tile, becomes its most-clicked.

The 'Greece → Home' through-line. One recurring photographic device — a single full-bleed Aegean-or-NJ image opening each page — becomes the brand thread across the whole site, so the OWNABLE element (their photography) is the consistent signature and ornament disappears entirely. The visual narrative mirrors the real one: he flew across the world to a Greek island; now they're getting married in New Jersey.

pageConcepts.concept: Magazine cover that orients then seduces: masthead + one-line facts caption, a single full-bleed Aegean establishing-shot hero, a tightened facts band ('Friday, June 18, 2027' · Whitehouse Station, NJ · days-only countdown · RSVP button · one voiced welcome line), a short 'Know Before You Go' teaser, the lightly-polished love story with a large Cormorant pull-quote divider, the kiss/reaction/detail frames as payoff, and an exit-ramp link block. Florals gone, accent eyedropped from the dome.

Curate it into a 'table,' not a list. Lead the grid with the most beautiful objects (Le Creuset, Pillivuyt, glassware) under the gold 'most wanted' star; relocate purely utilitarian items (the O-Cedar spin mop, basic storage) into a single lower group so the top reads desirable and on-brand. Add a one-line voiced registry intro. Most importantly, surface the HONEYMOON FUND near the top: give it a Mediterranean photo, the title 'Send Us Back to the Aegean,' and a 2–3 sentence description in their voice ('We got engaged on a clifftop in the Cyclades at sunset — and we're not done with the Greek islands yet. Your gift helps send us back to the Aegean as a married couple, and yes, we'll find that little blue-domed church again.'). Optionally split it into 2–3 concrete experience tiles, which convert better than one open pool. Consider 3–5 non-kitchen items and a couple more 'most wanted' stars across price points. Grid chrome is Zola-templated and not stylable — curation and copy are the levers.

Open with one full-bleed image (the through-line device). Present the Friday-evening weekend as a clean editorial itinerary in Cormorant time-stamps and sans descriptions, sentence-case, lots of air. Frame it warmly for a weekday wedding: a simple vertical timeline that tells out-of-towners exactly how the evening (and any Thursday-arrival or morning-after moments) will flow. Pure Zola text/schedule blocks.

The hospitality page a Friday countryside wedding needs. Lead image, then plainspoken sections: nearest airport (Newark) and rough drive time, hotel blocks once set, and — a chance to let their voice shine — a short, charming 'what to do in Hunterdon County' list. Could carry a quiet '(Meghan:) practical answer / (Owen:) romantic answer' device to seed the dual-voice idea.

Editorial portrait gallery: each person a clean photo plus a one-or-two-line caption in the couple's voice. This is the natural home to honor the real supporting cast of the proposal story — Sophia, Sophia's mom (the day-long co-conspirator), and Edie, the roommate whose boyfriend started the whole butterfly effect — turning names from the story into faces. Consistent crop discipline across portraits.

The page Quiet Editorial is built for — and the place to give the proposal photography the range it currently lacks. Art-direct it as two acts: Act I 'The Aegean' (wide establishing island shots, the dome, the clifftop, details, the kiss), Act II 'Home' (everyday-couple and Whitehouse Station / NJ imagery), sequenced wide→tight within each. Add a small 'How he pulled it off' sequence — the 36-hour journey, the co-conspirators — so guests arrive already in on the romance. Full-bleed, no shadows, no borders.

The findability safety net, written in their warm, funny voice (the love-story copy proves they can). Group by theme — the Friday timing, dress code, kids/plus-ones, parking, getting there — leading with the highest-frequency questions. Could carry the dual-voice device (her dry practical answer, his warm one). Reduces load on every other page and on the couple's inbox. Pure content in Zola.

concepts.copyVoice: Two named voices, used as an editorial system rather than a single buried paragraph. Meghan: hilarious, organized, dry, unapologetically type-A. Owen: calm, go-with-the-flow, endlessly romantic, just as funny. The love-story copy already proves this register exists — the job is to let it 'leak,' deliberately and sparingly, into every place a guest's eye lands instead of quarantining it in 150 words. Concrete voice moves: (1) Replace generic labels with voiced ones where it helps — the story section could become 'The Easiest Yes' or 'Five Years In' (echoing their own line); the honeymoon fund becomes 'Send Us Back to the Aegean.' (2) A one-line voiced welcome under the masthead: 'We're getting married — and somehow we talked an entire Greek island into helping ask.' (3) On Travel/FAQs later, an optional recurring '(Meghan:) … (Owen:) …' aside — she answers practically and dryly, he answers warmly — so the dual portrait from paragraph two gets performed, not just described. Copyediting hygiene (a type-A bride will wince otherwise): fix 'Meghan's roommate,' 'Meghan's friends,' 'Sophia's mom'; split paragraph one's long sentence; use contractions throughout; name Edie's role at first mention so the full-circle payoff lands. The rule: the design is silent and restrained so the WORDS carry the personality — every heading, button, and microcopy line should sound like the two people who wrote 'the easiest yes of all time,' never like default Zola ('For all the days along the way').
concepts.feasibilityOnZola: Mostly settings, content, and curation — with one honest stretch. FULLY FEASIBLE TODAY (no code, no theme change): enabling the native RSVP page and adding it to the nav; changing the theme accent color to the Aegean hex #1E5F8C; keeping Cormorant + Circular (both in Zola's font set); all copy edits, voiced microcopy, and the apostrophe/sentence fixes; reordering hero photo blocks and using a single full-bleed image; surfacing names/date/location higher; switching the countdown to days-only IF the theme exposes that toggle (flag: countdown granularity and whether it can be hidden are theme-dependent); all registry curation — reordering, the 'most wanted' stars, adding the honeymoon-fund photo/title/description, splitting it into experience tiles, broadening the item mix; building Schedule/Travel/Wedding Party/Gallery/FAQs from Zola text, photo, gallery, and schedule blocks; the typographic pull-quote and ampersand 'mark' (they're just large text in blocks — Zola won't allow a custom CSS divider, but it fully controls text blocks). THE ONE STRETCH — and it's load-bearing for this direction: removing the persistent blush watercolor florals. Per the dossier these are baked into the template art and generally cannot be recolored or removed individually in Zola. Because Quiet Editorial's entire premise is near-zero ornament, this direction most likely requires a THEME SWITCH to a clean, photo-forward, minimal/no-ornament Zola theme. Flag clearly: a theme switch will reset some layout and design tokens, so re-check the hero, type sizes, color application, nav active-state, and the registry after switching, and confirm Cormorant/Circular and a custom accent hex survive in the new theme. HARD LIMITS to accept: no arbitrary HTML/CSS, so true edge-to-edge bleed, exact margins, and overlay text on the hero all depend on what the chosen theme natively supports (some themes allow a hero caption/overlay; if not, the under-hero facts band does the orientation work instead); the registry grid chrome (toolbar, filter row, card layout) is Zola-templated and not stylable — only curation and copy are controllable.
concepts.risks: Honest tradeoffs. (1) The theme switch is the big one. It's the cleanest path to the floral-free, photo-forward look this direction needs, but it resets layout and risks losing the exact Cormorant/sage/whitespace tuning they've already dialed in — they should audition a candidate theme on a copy/preview, budget an afternoon to re-check every page, and be ready to walk it back if a clean theme can't keep Cormorant + the custom accent. If they're unwilling to switch themes, this direction degrades gracefully: keep the current theme, recolor the accent to Aegean blue, lean hard on blue-white photography so the florals recede, and accept the florals as a compromise — but be clear that's a diluted version, not full Quiet Editorial. (2) Photographic monoculture. Quiet Editorial leans the entire identity on imagery, but right now one Sifnos set is doing all the work and repeats across pages. Without a broader photo program (engagement-style portraits, NJ/home imagery, details, the gallery's two acts), 'restraint' can tip into 'sparse' or 'repetitive.' This direction needs them to gather more images over the 13-month runway. (3) Minimalism's thin line. Maximum white space and near-zero ornament read as 'expensive and considered' when the photos are strong and the type is tuned — and as 'unfinished' when they aren't. The countermeasure is exactly the discipline above: strong full-bleed images, the days-only countdown and date set with real weight, and the pull-quote/ampersand giving the eye authored rest stops so the page never feels empty. (4) Accent-on-white legibility. Mid-blue Cormorant at small sizes on white can dip under WCAG AA; the harbor-navy fallback (#103A52) and the rule 'large display only in blue, everything small in near-black' protect older guests reading on a phone in daylight — but it must actually be enforced, not assumed. (5) Countdown dependency. If the theme can't reduce the countdown to days-only or hide it, a ticking-seconds timer slightly undercuts the editorial calm — at minimum make the date line dominant so the timer reads as a footnote. None of these are dealbreakers; they're the cost of committing fully to letting the photography and the white space carry the wedding.

concepts.tagline: Engaged on a Greek cliff. Married in the Jersey countryside. You're invited to the whole story.
concepts.bigIdea:
Meghan and Owen already proved they're extraordinary hosts — they got an entire Greek island to conspire in a surprise proposal. This direction makes the website behave the way they behave: warm, funny, organized, and three steps ahead of every guest's question. The site stops being a beautiful keepsake that happens to mention a wedding and becomes a genuine host standing at the door — greeting you by saying who/when/where in the couple's own voice, then walking you through everything you need to feel taken care of for a Friday wedding in rural New Jersey.

The organizing principle is a paired voice the love-story copy already establishes but never reuses: Meghan's type-A wit and Owen's calm romance. We turn that two-person voice into a site-wide SYSTEM — a recurring "(Meghan:) / (Owen:)" aside used sparingly on practical pages (FAQs, Schedule, Travel) where she answers crisply and he answers warmly. That single device does what no floral can: it makes every page sound unmistakably like THEM, turns logistics into hospitality, and gives the whole site a personality spine that costs nothing but copy.

Crucially, we resolve the panel's biggest shared note — the warm English-cottage florals fighting the cool Cycladic photography — by letting the Aegean become the brand. We retire sage as the accent and pull a cobalt straight from the blue-domed Church of the Seven Martyrs. Now the color of the words is the color of the place Owen proposed, the photography stops fighting the decoration, and "The Warmest Hosts" gets a look as specific as its voice: sun, sea, whitewashed stone, and two people who clearly love taking care of the people they love.
concepts.visualLanguage:
Editorial-Mediterranean hospitality. Keep the genuinely sophisticated typographic spine the panel praised (Cormorant Garamond display + clean sans body, masthead restraint, generous whitespace, narrow reading measure) but swap the emotional temperature from cool-austere to warm-hosted. The shift is driven by three levers, all reachable in Zola: COLOR, PHOTOGRAPHY RHYTHM, and VOICE.

1) Let the Aegean be the brand. Re-pull the single Zola accent color from the couple's own hero — a cobalt eyedropped from the church dome — so headings, links, buttons, and dividers all read as "the color of Sifnos." This is the move five of six reviewers converged on; it reconciles the photos and the template in one setting change.

2) Photography as the recurring signature, not the florals. Today one proposal set repeats 4+ times while the generic floral repeats on every page — exactly backwards (Theo). Invert it: make ONE full-bleed, consistently-cropped Mediterranean image the through-line at the top of every page (home hero, registry, and each future page header), and dial the florals down to a single quiet placement. The brand thread becomes their light and their sea, evolving page to page, instead of borrowed blooms.

3) Warmth via voice and white space, not clutter. Pure white stays (it lets the blue-white photos pop), but we break the "islands of photo floating in white" problem by adding deliberate full-bleed photo bands lower on pages so the Aegean color reappears in a rhythm: photo / white / photo. The hosting personality lives in microcopy, pull-quotes, and a friendly "here's what you need" block — charm and clarity over visual austerity.

The feeling on arrival: you've been personally welcomed by two people who are clearly good at this, the place they fell in love is the color of the whole site, and you already know it's a Friday and roughly how the weekend works before you've asked a single question.
palette: #1F6FB2 — Aegean Cobalt (primary accent; eyedropped from the church dome — set as the Zola theme color; headings, links, buttons, active nav)
#0E0E0E — Ink (body + UI text; keep for contrast and legibility, especially critical guest info)
#FFFFFF — Whitewash (page background; lets blue-white photography breathe)
#F4EFE6 — Warm Stone / Cream (optional soft band tint where the theme allows a section background, to add hosted warmth and break up pure white)
#E4B45E — Sunset Gold (tiny doses only — keep on the existing 'Our most wanted' registry star and as a sparing pull-quote/accent; echoes the proposal's golden-hour light)

#7C8B86 — Retired Sage (note: stepping back from sage as the lead accent — it ties the palette to the template florals rather than the couple's story; may persist in baked-in floral art, which is the case for a theme switch)
concepts.typography:
Keep the pairing the panel called correct and confident — no need to fight Zola here. Display: Cormorant Garamond (a Zola-available elegant serif) for the wordmark, H1s, names, date, section titles, and pull-quotes — all-caps with ~2px tracking reserved for SHORT strings only (wordmark, page titles, names). Body/UI: the clean default sans (Circular-style) at ~16px in Ink #0E0E0E for all paragraphs, nav, buttons, and — importantly — all critical guest information.

Three disciplined changes the reviewers flagged: (1) Never set sentence-length or paragraph copy in all-caps Cormorant — caps are for labels and names, never the running voice (the love story is already sans; keep it). (2) Stop shrinking the thin display serif to body size; any sub-heading or label below roughly H3 falls back to the near-black sans rather than tiny tracked Cormorant, which softens on phones in sunlight. (3) Fix the hierarchy weak point — the date and countdown. Render the date as a confident Cormorant line ("Friday, June 18, 2027") as the visual anchor of the names band, and demote the live timer beneath it (or simplify to days-only) in readable, full-weight sans rather than small light gray. Moving the accent from sage to Aegean cobalt also improves heading contrast on white while tying the type color to the photography. Net: the editorial poise stays; the legibility and the personality both go up.
concepts.homepage:
Reordered so the site hosts before it dazzles — the kiss becomes a payoff, not the opening line. Top to bottom:

1) WORDMARK + NAV (white, as today) — now with one small voiced tagline beneath the wordmark where the theme allows a text block: "Engaged on a Greek cliff · married in the Jersey countryside." First words a guest reads finally sound like them.

2) HERO — keep the bold full-bleed band, but RESEQUENCE so it builds: lead with the wide clifftop/ocean establishing shot (the blue-domed church, the Aegean), then her reaction, and let the kiss RESOLVE at the right edge (Zola reorders photo blocks). The image still carries the top with no overlaid script — the restraint the panel liked stays.

3) WELCOME + THE FACTS (new, immediately under the hero) — a short, warm hosted block so who/when/where clears the fold without a long scroll, especially on mobile. Names in Cormorant, then a confident "FRIDAY, JUNE 18, 2027 · WHITEHOUSE STATION, NJ," then one voiced welcome line: "We're getting married — and yes, we talked an entire Greek island into helping ask. We'd love you with us in the Jersey countryside; come make a weekend of it." Then the home page's single PRIMARY CTA in cobalt: "RSVP" (pre-open: "RSVPs open early 2027 — hold the date"), with quieter secondary links to Travel and Schedule. This is the keystone fix the conversion/UX/planner reviewers all demanded.

4) AT-A-GLANCE STRIP — a compact 4-up "lobby directory" answering the 5-second job: Date (Fri, Jun 18, 2027) · Place (Whitehouse Station, NJ) · Getting here (~X from Newark — make a Friday/weekend of it) · RSVP by [date]. Screenshot-and-go for out-of-towners; Zola text/columns blocks.

5) OUR STORY — keep the genuinely excellent copy, lightly polished (see copy voice). Retitle from the generic "OUR LOVE STORY" to "THE EASIEST YES" (their own line). Lift one gold-standard sentence into a large Cormorant PULL-QUOTE between the prose and the photos — "It was the easiest yes of all time." — so skimmers get rewarded and the page feels art-directed by the people who lived it.

6) PROPOSAL PHOTOS — cut the redundancy the photo director flagged: don't repeat the same clifftop frames used in the hero. Keep ONE strong placement and vary the register (a detail — the ring/hands — or a quiet wide island landscape) so the page has texture, not echo.

7) EXIT RAMP (new) — at the warmest moment, right after the story, a closing block with 2-3 inline cobalt links: "RSVP" · "Plan your trip" · "See the registry." Capture intent at peak emotion instead of dumping into the footer.

8) FOOTER — Zola standard; if a closing text block is allowed, one last voiced line.
concepts.navAndIA:
Resolve the missing RSVP — the unanimous top priority — and make the who/when/where reachable in one screen.

NAV: Add the native Zola RSVP page and place it as the rightmost, visually-emphasized item so it reads as the terminal action: Home | Schedule | Travel | Registry | Wedding Party | Gallery | FAQs | RSVP. Pre-invitations, the RSVP page holds a dated promise ("RSVPs open early 2027 — please hold Friday, June 18") so the funnel and guest expectation exist now. Keep the underlined active state but verify it's distinguishable; use a bolder active treatment if the theme offers one.

EIGHT ITEMS / MOBILE: Eight is past the comfortable single-row limit, so pressure-test the mobile hamburger order and ensure it leads with RSVP and Home. If it ever crowds, Gallery is the most deferrable top-level item once content exists. ("Schedule" is fine; it's a clear label.)

WHO/WHEN/WHERE: Lives in three places now, not one buried band — (a) the small wordmark tagline, (b) the Welcome + Facts block directly under the hero, and (c) the At-a-Glance strip. The date renders everywhere as "Friday, June 18, 2027" so the load-bearing weekday fact is impossible to miss, and the venue name (not just the town) joins the facts once known.

HOSPITALITY IA: The home page also seeds a "here's roughly how the weekend works" teaser (a built-page content add, not a critique of empty pages) so a Friday rural-NJ wedding feels guided from day one and inbound logistics questions drop.
signatureMoments: The two-voice aside, used site-wide. A recurring '(Meghan:) … / (Owen:) …' device where she answers practically and dryly, he answers warmly — sprinkled sparingly across FAQs, Schedule, and Travel. It performs the personalities the love story only names, turns logistics into charm, and is the cheapest, most ownable brand thread on the site (pure Zola text). Example FAQ — Q: Can we bring the kids? (Meghan:) We adore them; this one's a grown-ups' night. (Owen:) Treat yourselves to a date night on us.

The Aegean accent: the color of the words is the color of where he proposed. Re-pulling the single theme color to a cobalt from the church dome so every heading, button, and link visually rhymes with the photography. The instant a guest who's seen the photos lands on any page, the brand feels like Sifnos, not a stock template.

'The Easiest Yes' pull-quote moment. Lifting their best line into a large Cormorant quote between the story prose and the photos — a deliberate breath that rewards skimmers and makes the home page feel authored by the couple, not the template.

The honeymoon fund as the story's third act: 'Send Us Back to the Aegean.' Re-titled, re-positioned near the top of the registry, given a Mediterranean photo and a voiced description that closes the narrative loop (met by butterfly effect → engaged on Sifnos → returning married). Turns the one cash tile from blank default into the most clickable, most-shared item on the page.

The Welcome + Facts + RSVP block under the hero — the host at the door. One warm sentence, the Friday date stated plainly, and a single cobalt RSVP button. It converts emotional momentum into the one action the couple actually needs and answers a guest's first three questions before they ask.

pageConcepts.concept: Reordered to host-then-dazzle: tagline under the wordmark, a hero that BUILDS to the kiss, then a Welcome + Facts + RSVP block (Friday date stated, cobalt CTA), an At-a-Glance strip, the polished story retitled 'The Easiest Yes' with a pull-quote, one non-redundant photo, and a warm exit-ramp of links. Charm and clarity above the fold; the keepsake beauty stays, but it now serves the guest.

Same Zola grid chrome, smarter curation and voice. Add a one-line voiced intro ('Truthfully, we mostly just want you there — but if you're inclined, here's what would make our first home feel like ours'). Lead the grid with the most beautiful objects (Le Creuset, Pillivuyt, the glassware), keep the gold 'most wanted' star, and tuck purely utilitarian basics (the spin mop, plain storage) into a single lower group. Most importantly: pull the honeymoon fund UP near the top, re-title it 'Send Us Back to the Aegean,' give it a Mediterranean photo and a 2-3 sentence voiced description, and consider splitting it into named experience tiles ('a sunset dinner on the cliffs,' 'a day on the water'). Optional warmth: a few non-kitchen entry points (a date-night fund, a hosting piece, a cookbook they love).

OPPORTUNITY: the hosted weekend itinerary for a Friday wedding. A clean Cormorant timeline (Welcome drinks → Ceremony → Reception → a casual Saturday send-off if there is one), each line with times, the venue name, and a dress-code note. Lace in the two-voice asides for tone — (Meghan:) Ceremony starts at 5:00 sharp, please beat us there. (Owen:) Then we eat, we dance, we don't stop. A full-bleed Mediterranean-toned header image keeps the brand thread.

OPPORTUNITY: the out-of-towner concierge for rural Hunterdon County. Nearest airport + rough drive time, the hotel block(s) with booking links, a 'rent a car — you'll want one out here' note, and a short 'make a weekend of it' list of local things to do. Frame the Friday logistics warmly (Thursday-night or Friday-morning arrival). Two-voice asides shine here — (Meghan:) Book the block early; rooms go fast. (Owen:) Or come a day early and explore — it's beautiful country.

OPPORTUNITY: the place to pay off the love story's cast. Short, warm intros for each person — and a built-in callback the story sets up: Sophia (the 'I'm going to marry him' confidante) and Edie (the roommate whose chain of events started it all and who was THERE for the proposal). One charming line each in the couple's voice turns a name list into the people who made this happen. Consistent photo crops carry the brand.

OPPORTUNITY: art-direct as two chapters — 'Greece → Home.' Act I: the Cycladic proposal world (wide island establishing shots, the dome, the cliff, the kiss, details — ring, hands, the Aegean), optionally sequenced as 'How he pulled it off' (the 36-hour journey, Sophia's mom as co-conspirator). Act II: their everyday life and imagery tying to Whitehouse Station and the June wedding. Sequence wide→tight within each act so it reads as a told story, not a photo dump.

OPPORTUNITY: the findability safety net AND the showcase for the two-voice device. Group by theme, lead with highest-frequency questions (Friday timing, dress code, kids, plus-ones, parking, what to do in the area), and answer the human ones as paired asides — she crisp, he warm. This is the single highest-ROI page for cutting guest confusion and inbox load, and it makes practical info feel like hospitality.

concepts.copyVoice:
Two people, one warm host. The love story already proves the register — specific, funny, human, never mushy — so we systematize it rather than reinvent it. Default voice: warm and conversational, in contractions (the site currently spells out 'did not / almost did not,' which reads stiff against the chattier story — switch to 'didn't / wasn't'). Signature device: the paired '(Meghan:) / (Owen:)' aside on practical pages — she's organized, dry, type-A; he's calm, go-with-the-flow, endlessly romantic. Used sparingly, it's the personality spine of the whole site.

Quick wins the writer flagged, all in Zola text blocks: (1) Proofread the love story's possessives — 'Meghan's roommate,' 'Meghan's friends,' 'Sophia's mom' (a type-A bride will wince at missing apostrophes in the most-read passage). (2) Split paragraph one's long breath-holding sentence so the gold line — 'I am going to marry him. Five years later, here we are' — lands cleanly. (3) Name Edie's role at first mention so paragraph three can pay it off as a full-circle moment ('Edie — the roommate who introduced us — was right there when he proposed'). (4) Retitle the story section 'The Easiest Yes.'

Microcopy principles: every place a guest's eye lands should sound like the people who wrote the proposal paragraph — the welcome line, the registry intro, the honeymoon-fund description, FAQ answers, even the date framing ('We're getting married on a Friday — come make a weekend of it'). Honeymoon-fund copy: 'We got engaged at sunset on a clifftop in the Cyclades — and we're not done with the Greek islands yet. Help send us back to the Aegean as a married couple (yes, we'll find that little blue-domed church again).' Warm, specific, theirs — and it plants the Aegean thread the whole design leans on.
concepts.feasibilityOnZola:
Almost everything here ships inside Zola with settings, page toggles, photo choices, content, and copy — no custom code.

FULLY FEASIBLE (do this weekend): enable the native RSVP page and add it to the nav; reorder hero/photo blocks; add Welcome+Facts, At-a-Glance, pull-quote, and exit-ramp via text/columns/photo and button blocks; re-pull the theme accent color to a custom cobalt hex (Zola supports custom theme color); render the date as 'Friday, June 18, 2027'; demote/simplify the countdown if the theme allows; retitle sections; rewrite all copy and microcopy including the two-voice asides; proofread the story; curate and reorder the registry, re-title and re-position the honeymoon fund, add its photo + description and optional experience tiles; add full-bleed photo bands lower on pages; add per-page Mediterranean header images as the recurring brand thread.

DEPENDS ON THEME (verify, no code): whether a text block can sit directly under the wordmark for the tagline; whether the hero supports any caption/overlay; countdown granularity/visibility toggles; how aggressively the mobile nav collapses with eight items; whether a section can take the warm-stone tint or a closing footer text block.

PLATFORM LIMITS / FLAG HONESTLY: the watercolor florals are baked into the template art — individual recoloring/removal generally isn't possible in Zola without changing themes, and you can't upload custom CSS dividers or arbitrary HTML. Re-pulling the accent to cobalt and leaning on blue-white photography narrows the palette clash a lot even while the warm florals remain. If, after the accent change, the persistent corner floral still actively fights the photography, a one-time theme switch to a cleaner/photo-forward (ideally cooler/Mediterranean) Zola theme is the only way to fully retire it — re-check layout afterward, since a switch can reset some structure. That's the single biggest 'maybe' here; everything else is native.
concepts.risks:
VOICE OVERUSE IS THE MAIN RISK. The '(Meghan:) / (Owen:)' device is delightful in small doses and exhausting in large ones — if it appears on every line it becomes a gimmick and undercuts the elegance. Rule: a few asides per page, only where a human touch adds warmth (FAQs, Schedule, Travel); never on the masthead, the date, or critical logistics, which stay clean and clear. Charm must not cost clarity — the whole direction is hospitality-first, so legibility and findability always win the tie.

THEME-SWITCH TRADEOFF. The cleanest palette fix (retiring the florals entirely) may require a theme switch, which can reset layout and is more work than a settings change. Mitigation: try the cobalt-accent + photo-forward approach first within the current theme; only switch if the floral clash still reads as 'borrowed' afterward. Don't let a perfect palette delay the keystone fixes (RSVP, above-fold facts) that need no switch at all.

PHOTOGRAPHY DEPTH. Making photography the brand thread assumes a broader image set than one proposal location; today it's one place shown 4+ times. Mitigation: vary register now (details, wide landscapes) and plan a small additional shoot — including some Jersey/home imagery — so the recurring full-bleed headers don't become the new monotony. If a deep set never materializes, lean harder on type and the warm-stone band so pages don't feel thin.

ACCESSIBILITY VIGILANCE. Guests skew older and mobile; cobalt on white must stay above AA (it should, and it beats sage), but keep all critical info in full-weight near-black sans, never thin tracked Cormorant or light gray. Re-check the countdown and small labels on a real phone in daylight.

SCOPE/RUNWAY. Eight nav items and several forward-looking pages is a lot to fill; with 13 months there's time, but sequence it — RSVP + above-fold facts + honeymoon-fund copy first (highest ROI, lowest effort), then the voice system and page build-out. The biggest non-design risk is simply leaving the keystone guest-task fixes unshipped while polishing the look.

evaluations.comment: From my seat this is the most reliable hosting upgrade of the three, because it lands every keystone guest fix WITHOUT betting the direction on a platform stretch. It enables a native RSVP page and puts it rightmost in the nav; it surfaces who/when/where in three places (optional wordmark tagline, an orientation line directly under the hero, and the names band) so a phone guest answers 'when/where/how do I respond' without a deliberate scroll; it renders the date as 'Friday, June 18, 2027' so the load-bearing weekday fact is unmissable; and it reframes the blank honeymoon tile into a described, photo-backed 'Send Us Back to the Aegean' moment near the top of the registry. The 'At a glance' guest card is exactly the lobby-directory move I want for out-of-town guests on phones. On craft: keeping the praised Cormorant/Circular type system and only recoloring the accent to a WCAG-checked mid-deep blue (#2E6F95/#1B4965 both clear AA on white, beating sage) is the right discipline — it improves daylight phone legibility while making the wordmark finally belong to the photography. The honest stance on the floral clash is its biggest strength FOR guests: it does the no-regrets accent + curation move first and only treats a theme switch as a deliberate, eyes-open last resort, so the guest-critical fixes are never held hostage to a layout-resetting change. Where I dock points: 'reduce hero height somewhat' and 'days-only countdown' are correctly flagged as theme-dependent, and the multi-panel hero still risks awkward mobile reflow even reduced — the orientation line mitigates but does not eliminate that, so it MUST be tested on a real phone. Authenticity is high (the place becomes the color, the proposal becomes the spine) but slightly behind Warmest Hosts because the voice-as-a-system idea is present yet not the organizing principle. Net: the safest, most complete host.

evaluations.concept: Quiet Editorial — "The Aegean, Set in Whitespace"
evaluations.comment: This is unquestionably the most beautiful direction and, on paper, the most editorial — and it does include the same correct keystone guest fixes (native RSVP rightmost; date as 'Friday, June 18, 2027'; a slim facts caption under the masthead PLUS a tightened facts band PLUS a 'Know Before You Go' strip; days-only countdown; the reframed honeymoon fund as Act III). The single-image hero is genuinely the most mobile-safe, orientation-first hero of the three, and I love that it stops spending the kiss as a spoiler. But I have to score from the guest's real-world experience and from risk, and two things hold it back hard. First, feasibility: the dossier itself admits the entire premise — near-zero ornament — most likely REQUIRES a theme switch to strip the baked-in florals, and a theme switch resets layout and design tokens and forces re-checking the hero, type sizes, color application, nav active-state, and registry. That means the platform's one big stretch sits UPSTREAM of the keystone fixes guests depend on; if the switch goes sideways or a clean theme can't preserve Cormorant + a custom accent, the couple risks shipping a half-migrated site. The right order of operations is fixes-first, beauty-second, and this direction inverts it. Second, the guest-experience risk of minimalism itself: 'maximum white space and near-zero ornament' reads as expensive ONLY when the photo set is deep — and today it is one Sifnos set repeated 4+ times. Until a broader cool-edited library exists, 'restraint' tips toward 'sparse/repetitive,' which to a guest can read as 'unfinished,' the exact impression we must avoid. The dossier's own graceful-degradation fallback (keep the theme, recolor accent, lean on blue-white photos) is essentially The Aegean Thread — which tells me the safe version of Quiet Editorial IS another concept. Visual craft is a 10 and authenticity is strong, but I cannot rate feasibility above a 5 when the direction is load-bearing on the one thing Zola can't reliably do, and guest experience takes a real hit from the photo-depth dependency.

This is the direction built around the exact thing I weight most: a site that BEHAVES like a host. It ships the same keystone fixes as Aegean Thread (native RSVP as the emphasized rightmost item with a dated pre-open promise; a Welcome + Facts + RSVP block directly under the hero so who/when/where and the one action clear the fold; an At-a-Glance 'lobby directory' 4-up; date everywhere as 'Friday, June 18, 2027'; the reframed 'Send Us Back to the Aegean' fund near the top of the registry; the same no-code-first accent move with theme switch only as a flagged last resort). What pushes it to a 10 on guest experience is the two-voice '(Meghan:)/(Owen:)' device deployed on FAQs, Schedule, and Travel — those are precisely the logistics pages where confusion lives for a Friday wedding in rural Hunterdon County, and turning a dry FAQ answer into a warm, clear paired answer is the single highest-ROI move for REDUCING guest confusion while increasing warmth. The worked FAQ example (kids: Meghan crisp 'grown-ups' night,' Owen warm 'date night on us') is exactly how you make a 'no' feel hospitable rather than cold. Feasibility is strong: nearly everything is native Zola copy/curation/settings, and the accent is a checked cobalt (#1F6FB2) that beats sage on white. I dock guest-experience nothing but I do flag the one real risk the concept itself names and guardrails well: voice overuse. If the asides creep onto the masthead, the date, or critical logistics, charm starts costing clarity — and clarity must always win the tie. The concept's own rule ('a few asides per page, never on the date or logistics') is the correct guardrail and must be enforced, not assumed. Visual craft matches Aegean Thread (same type system, same accent logic, adds warm-stone bands for hosted warmth). Authenticity edges ahead of both rivals because the ownable asset here isn't only the place — it's the two PEOPLE, performed site-wide, which is harder to fake than a color.

tensions.tension: Generic florals vs. the Mediterranean story — and HOW far to go to resolve it (accent-shift vs. theme switch).
tensions.position: This is the loudest shared note across all six critics and I agree the warm English-cottage florals are at war with the cool Cycladic photography. But as the guest advocate I care about the FIX ORDER more than the ideal end-state. The no-regrets move is unanimous and native: retire sage, eyedrop a mid-deep Aegean blue from the church dome as the Zola theme color, and lean hard on cool blue-white imagery so the photos carry the identity and the florals recede. That alone narrows the clash dramatically and ships this weekend with zero risk to guest-critical content. A theme switch — which Quiet Editorial REQUIRES and the other two treat as a last resort — resets layout and forces re-checking the RSVP, the facts band, the nav active-state, and the registry. My position: do accent + photo-forward curation FIRST, ship the keystone guest fixes immediately, judge the residual floral clash on a live phone, and only then decide on a deliberate, eyes-open theme switch. A theme change is a wager on Zola's weakest capability; never let that wager block the RSVP and the date from reaching guests. Guests notice a missing RSVP instantly; they will forgive a blush spray in the corner of a site that otherwise hosts them flawlessly.

I come down firmly on warmth-and-helpfulness, and this is where I diverge most from the panel's aesthetic enthusiasm. Quiet Editorial is the most gorgeous artifact, but a wedding site is a task tool first and a keepsake second, and maximum-whitespace minimalism is a high-wire act that only reads as 'expensive' when the photography is deep and varied. Today it is one Sifnos set shown 4+ times — exactly the 'monotonous ownable element' Theo flagged. Strip ornament to near-zero on top of a one-note photo library and 'restrained' tips into 'sparse' or 'unfinished' to a guest, which is the precise wrong signal. Warmth scales better than austerity on the assets this couple actually has TODAY: the two-voice device, voiced microcopy, an orientation line, and an at-a-glance card add hospitality and clarity using copy they can write tonight, with no dependency on a future shoot.
